3139	% $$
3140	% as $R'$-modules."
3141	% NOTE: This affine lemma is closed directly on global sections, via the two named
3142	% obligations: lem:base_change_map_affine_local (locality reduction on the affine
3143	% opens of S') and lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange (the section-
3144	% level value of the base-change map is cancelBaseChange). Both proved affine tilde
3145	% dictionaries -- lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso and lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso --
3146	% feed the second obligation. cancelBaseChange is in Mathlib with no flatness; the
3147	% affine case carries no flatness hypothesis. No Čech infrastructure is used here.
3148	\begin{proof}
3149	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:base_change_map_affine_local, lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
3150	  The proof is the composite of the two named obligations, taken \emph{directly on
3151	  global sections}.
3152	
3153	  \medskip
3154	  \emph{First reduction (locality on \(S'\)).} By
3155	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
3156	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
3157	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). This is exactly the
3158	  locality reduction Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}: restricting the
3159	  cartesian square to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R') \subseteq S'\) and
3160	  a chosen affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing \(g(U)\), the
3161	  hypothesis \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to
3162	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict
3163	  to \(\widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that quasi-coherence is
3164	  used: it is what licenses writing \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\).) Computing the
3165	  fibre product gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\) and
3166	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) the tilde of the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module
3167	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). The matter is thus reduced to the affine--affine
3168	  model, where the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
3169	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
3170	
3171	  \medskip
3172	  \emph{Passage to global sections.} The functor
3173	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
3174	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful with essential image
3175	  the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently the counit
3176	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
3177	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
3178	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms and, by their naturality,
3179	  \[
3180	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
3181	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
3182	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
3183	  \]
3184	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map in
3185	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). The whole computation therefore stays at the level
3186	  of modules.
3187	
3188	  \medskip
3189	  \emph{Section-level identification (the second obligation).} By the section-level
3190	  computation Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, the map
3191	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\), with its domain \(\Gamma(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M}))\) identified with
3192	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) and its codomain \(\Gamma(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M})\) identified
3193	  with \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) through the two dictionaries
3194	  Lemmas~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso},
3195	  \emph{is} the canonical cancellation isomorphism
3196	  \[
3197	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
3198	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M,
3199	    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)
3200	  \]
3201	  of \(R'\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}). This is the entire
3202	  content of the affine close, and it is performed \emph{directly on the module of
3203	  global sections} — there is no separate abstract adjoint-mate identification at the
3204	  level of sheaves. Because \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with
3205	  no flatness hypothesis whatsoever, \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is an isomorphism, hence so is
3206	  \(\alpha\) on the affine chart, and by the locality reduction so is the global
3207	  base-change map. Flatness of \(g\) plays no role in the affine lemma; it enters only
3208	  in the global statement Theorem~\ref{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}, where it is
3209	  used to commute \(- \otimes_A B\) with the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\).
3210	\end{proof}

3212	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
3213	
3214	The global statement is assembled from the affine lemma and the single homological
3215	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
3216	supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.

3218	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
3219	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
3220	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
3221	  \mathlibok
3222	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3223	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
3224	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
3225	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
3226	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
3227	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
3228	  \[
3229	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
3230	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
3231	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
3232	  \]
3233	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
3234	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
3235	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
3236	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
3237	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
3238	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
3239	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
3240	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
3241	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
3242	\end{lemma}

3244	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf condition as an equalizer of products]
3245	  \label{lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib}
3246	  \lean{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafEqualizerProducts}
3247	  \mathlibok
3248	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3249	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a presheaf with values in a category with products on a
3250	  topological space, and let \(\mathcal{U} = \{U_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of
3251	  opens with union \(U = \bigcup_i U_i\). Then \(\mathcal{F}\) satisfies the sheaf
3252	  condition on \(\mathcal{U}\) if and only if the fork
3253	  \[
3254	    \mathcal{F}(U)
3255	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathrm{res}\;}\;
3256	    \prod_i \mathcal{F}(U_i)
3257	    \;\underset{\mathrm{rightRes}}{\overset{\mathrm{leftRes}}{\rightrightarrows}}\;
3258	    \prod_{(i,j)} \mathcal{F}(U_i \cap U_j)
3259	  \]
3260	  is a limit (an equalizer), where the two parallel maps restrict the \(U_i\)- and
3261	  \(U_j\)-components to \(U_i \cap U_j\). This is Mathlib's
3262	  \texttt{isSheaf\_iff\_isSheafEqualizerProducts} together with the fork
3263	  \texttt{SheafConditionEqualizerProducts.fork}; it is exactly Čech degree
3264	  \(0 \to 1\) and requires no separatedness.
3265	\end{lemma}

3267	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3268	[Finite affine cover with quasi-compact overlaps]
3269	  \label{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3270	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact}
3271	  A quasi-compact scheme \(X\) admits a \emph{finite} affine open cover
3272	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\). If moreover \(X\) is
3273	  quasi-separated, then each pairwise intersection \(U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j\) is
3274	  quasi-compact; and if \(X\) is separated, each \(U_{ij}\) is in fact affine.
3275	  \begin{proof}
3276	    The affine opens form a basis, so \(X\) is covered by affine opens; quasi-compactness
3277	    extracts a finite subcover \(U_1, \dots, U_t\) (by Mathlib's affine cover and the
3278	    finite-subcover property of a quasi-compact space). Quasi-separatedness says the
3279	    intersection of two quasi-compact opens is quasi-compact, so each \(U_{ij}\) is
3280	    quasi-compact. When \(X\) is separated the diagonal is a closed immersion, whence
3281	    the intersection of two affine opens is affine (Mathlib, for separated schemes).
3282	  \end{proof}
3283	\end{lemma}

3293	  % now has its own block, Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer_cover}.

3296	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Fix a finite affine cover \(\mathcal{U} = \{U_i\}_{1 \le i \le t}\)
3297	  of \(X\) as in Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}. Then the module of global
3298	  sections is the equalizer of the two restriction maps of the cover,
3299	  \[
3300	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})

3354	The separated-case flat base change Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_separated} can be organised by
3355	presenting \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})\) as an honest \(\operatorname{LinearMap.eqLocus}\) over the global
3356	ring \(A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) and invoking the Mathlib base-change-of-equalizer equivalence
3357	\(\operatorname{tensorEqLocusEquiv}\) (recorded as the dependency anchor
3358	Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}, which pins exactly
3359	\texttt{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}). This sub-lane is independent of the affine section-level
3360	computation; its three ingredients are recorded here as build-ahead blocks. The Lean pins below name
3361	to-be-built declarations.

3363	\begin{lemma}[$A$-module structure on cover sections by restriction of scalars]
3364	  \label{lem:gamma_amodule_restriction}
3365	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.gammaCoverRestrictScalars}
3366	  \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer_cover}
3367	  Let \(X\) be a quasi-compact, quasi-separated scheme, \(M : X.\mathrm{Modules}\), and write
3368	  \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) for the ring of global sections of the structure sheaf. For a finite
3369	  affine cover \(\{U_i\}\) with overlaps \(U_{ij}\), the structure-sheaf restriction ring maps
3370	  \(A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(U_i,\mathcal{O}_X)\) and
3371	  \(A \to \Gamma(U_{ij},\mathcal{O}_X)\) make each module of sections \(\Gamma(U_i, M)\),
3372	  \(\Gamma(U_{ij}, M)\) an \(A\)-module by restriction of scalars; moreover the restriction maps of
3373	  \(M\) along inclusions of opens are \(\Gamma(X,-)\)-semilinear over those restriction ring maps,
3374	  hence \(A\)-linear after restriction of scalars.
3375	  \begin{proof}
3376	    \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer_cover}
3377	    Each \(\Gamma(U_i,M)\) is a module over \(\Gamma(U_i,\mathcal{O}_X)\); restricting scalars along
3378	    the ring map \(A \to \Gamma(U_i,\mathcal{O}_X)\) gives the \(A\)-module structure, and likewise on
3379	    \(U_{ij}\). A presheaf restriction of \(M\) over an inclusion \(V \subseteq U\) is additive and
3380	    semilinear over the corresponding restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\); since both the
3381	    \(\mathcal{O}_X\)- and \(M\)-restrictions are compatible with the maps out of the global sections
3382	    \(A\), the restriction becomes \(A\)-linear once scalars are restricted to \(A\).
3383	  \end{proof}

3408	\begin{lemma}[$\Gamma(X,M)$ as an $A$-linear equalizer locus]
3409	  \label{lem:gamma_eqLocus_iso}
3410	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.gammaEqLocusIso}
3411	  \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer_cover, lem:gamma_alinear_res_maps}
3412	  In the situation of Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_alinear_res_maps}, there is an \(A\)-linear isomorphism
3413	  \[
3414	    \Gamma(X, M) \;\cong\; \operatorname{eqLocus}(\mathrm{leftRes}, \mathrm{rightRes})
3415	      \;\subseteq\; \prod_i \Gamma(U_i, M),
3416	  \]
3417	  identifying the global sections with the submodule on which the two product restriction maps agree.
3418	  Combined with the base-change-of-equalizer equivalence
3419	  Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}, this presents flat base change of
3420	  \(\Gamma(X,M)\) as \(- \otimes_A B\) applied to the locus.
3421	  \begin{proof}
3422	    \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer_cover, lem:gamma_alinear_res_maps}
3423	    By Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer_cover} the global sections \(\Gamma(X,M)\) are the limit
3424	    of the sheaf-condition fork in abelian groups; the limit of a parallel pair is the equalizer,
3425	    whose underlying set is \(\{s : \mathrm{leftRes}\,s = \mathrm{rightRes}\,s\}\), i.e.\ the set-level
3426	    locus \(\operatorname{eqLocus}(\mathrm{leftRes},\mathrm{rightRes})\). The forgetful functor
3427	    \(\operatorname{forget}_2 : \operatorname{ModuleCat} A \to \mathbf{Ab}\) reflects this limit, so the
3428	    identification upgrades to an \(A\)-linear isomorphism with the \(A\)-module
3429	    \(\operatorname{eqLocus}\) of the \(A\)-linear maps of Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_alinear_res_maps}.
3430	  \end{proof}
3431	\end{lemma}

3433	\begin{lemma}[Base change of the finite equalizer diagram]
3434	  \label{lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram}
3435	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.baseChange_sheafConditionFork_tensorIso}
3436	  \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
3437	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH ($i=0$ case), separated paragraph
3438	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.md; quoted verbatim in the
3439	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF of thm:flat_base_change_pushforward below).
3440	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$
3441	  % we obtain by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$
3442	  % is affine and that $X_B$ is separated. ... We have the following relation
3443	  % between the {\v C}ech complexes
3444	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
3445	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B$
3446	  % as follows from Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}."
3447	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), $i=0$ case,
3448	  the relation \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B,\mathcal{F}_B) =
3449	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B\).}
3450	  Let \(A \to B\) be a ring map, \(X\) a separated scheme over \(A\) with finite
3451	  affine cover \(\mathcal{U} = \{U_i\}\) (so each \(U_{ij}\) is affine), and
3452	  \(\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent. Set \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec} A}
3453	  \operatorname{Spec} B\), \(\mathcal{F}_B\) the pullback, and
3454	  \(\mathcal{U}_B = \{(U_i)_B\}\). Then \(\mathcal{U}_B\) is a finite affine cover of
3455	  \(X_B\), and the sheaf-condition fork of Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer} for
3456	  \((X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) is obtained from that for \((X, \mathcal{F})\) by applying
3457	  \(- \otimes_A B\): there are compatible isomorphisms
3458	  \[
3459	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
3460	    \qquad
3461	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
3462	  \]
3463	  intertwining the two restriction maps, so that the whole diagram
3464	  \(\prod_i \Gamma((U_i)_B,\mathcal{F}_B) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j}
3465	  \Gamma((U_{ij})_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\) is \((- \otimes_A B)\) applied to
3466	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i,\mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j}
3467	  \Gamma(U_{ij},\mathcal{F})\).
3468	  \begin{proof}
3469	    \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
3470	    Base change preserves affineness and open immersions, so \((U_i)_B\) is affine and
3471	    \(\{(U_i)_B\}\) covers \(X_B\); since \(X\) is separated each \(U_{ij}\), hence each
3472	    \((U_{ij})_B\), is affine, and the fork of
3473	    Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer} applies to \((X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\). On each
3474	    affine term the global-sections form of the affine lemma
3475	    Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} supplies the isomorphism
3476	    \(\Gamma((U_i)_B,\mathcal{F}_B) \cong \Gamma(U_i,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B\)
3477	    (and likewise on the \(U_{ij}\)); these are natural in restriction, so they
3478	    intertwine \(\mathrm{leftRes}\) and \(\mathrm{rightRes}\). Finally
3479	    \(- \otimes_A B\) commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)
3480	    (Mathlib, tensor product preserves finite products), so the base-changed fork is
3481	    literally \((- \otimes_A B)\) applied to the original fork.
3482	  \end{proof}
3483	\end{lemma}

3485	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change, separated case]
3486	  \label{lem:flat_base_change_separated}
3487	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso_of_isSeparated}
3488	  \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer, lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram,
3489	        lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
3490	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH ($i=0$ case), separated paragraph
3491	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.md; quoted verbatim in the
3492	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF of thm:flat_base_change_pushforward below).
3493	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on
3494	  % taking cohomology."
3495	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), $i=0$ case,
3496	  separated paragraph.}
3497	  Let \(A \to B\) be flat, \(X\) a separated, quasi-compact scheme over \(A\), and
3498	  \(\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent. Then the comparison map
3499	  \[
3500	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\longrightarrow\; \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
3501	  \]
3502	  is an isomorphism.
3503	  \begin{proof}
3504	    \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer, lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram,
3505	          lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
3506	    By Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}, \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})\) and
3507	    \(\Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\) are the equalizers of the finite forks of the cover
3508	    \(\mathcal{U}\) and its base change \(\mathcal{U}_B\). By
3509	    Lemma~\ref{lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram} the \(X_B\)-fork is the
3510	    \(X\)-fork with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied. Since \(A \to B\) is flat,
3511	    \(- \otimes_A B\) commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
3512	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); hence
3513	    \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
3514	    \xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B) = \Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\),
3515	    and this iso is the comparison map.
3516	  \end{proof}
3517	\end{lemma}

3519	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change, Mayer--Vietoris reduction of the quasi-separated case]
3520	  \label{lem:flat_base_change_mayer_vietoris}
3521	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_mayerVietoris}
3522	  \uses{lem:flat_base_change_separated, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward,
3523	        lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib, lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3524	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH ($i=0$ case), quasi-separated paragraph
3525	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.md; quoted verbatim in the
3526	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF of thm:flat_base_change_pushforward below).
3527	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "In case $X$ is quasi-separated, choose an affine open
3528	  % covering $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$. ... The reader who
3529	  % wishes to avoid this spectral sequence can use Mayer-Vietoris and induction
3530	  % on $t$ as in the proof of Lemma
3531	  % \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}."
3532	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), $i=0$ case,
3533	  quasi-separated paragraph (Mayer--Vietoris induction on \(t\)).}
3534	  Let \(A \to B\) be flat, \(X\) a quasi-compact, quasi-separated scheme over \(A\),
3535	  and \(\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent. Then the comparison map
3536	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to \Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\) is an
3537	  isomorphism.
3538	  \begin{proof}
3539	    \uses{lem:flat_base_change_separated, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward,
3540	          lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib, lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3541	    Choose a finite affine cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\)
3542	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}) and induct on \(t\). For \(t = 1\),
3543	    \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the affine lemma
3544	    Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}. For \(t > 1\) put \(V = U_t\)
3545	    (affine) and \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (covered by \(t-1\) affines, hence
3546	    quasi-compact and quasi-separated). Their intersection \(W \cap V =
3547	    \bigcup_{i<t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the \(t-1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each
3548	    an open of the affine \(U_i\) and so quasi-compact and separated; thus \(W \cap V\)
3549	    is separated and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated
3550	    case Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_separated}. The two-member cover
3551	    \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris equalizer
3552	    \[
3553	      0 \to \Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \to
3554	        \Gamma(W,\mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V,\mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(W\cap V,\mathcal{F}),
3555	    \]
3556	    and likewise for \(X_B\) with \(\{W_B, V_B\}\). Flatness makes \(- \otimes_A B\)
3557	    preserve this finite kernel/equalizer
3558	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); since flat base change holds for
3559	    \(V\) (affine), for \(W\) (inductive hypothesis, \(t-1\) members), and for
3560	    \(W \cap V\) (separated), the five-lemma/equalizer comparison forces it for \(X\).
3561	  \end{proof}
3562	\end{lemma}

3564	\begin{lemma}[Reduction of the base-change map to the global-sections comparison]
3565	  \label{lem:flat_base_change_reduce_global_sections}
3566	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_isIso_iff_gammaTensorComparison}
3567	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
3568	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (the reduction "part (1) follows from
3569	  % part (2)") (read from references/stacks-coherent.md; quoted verbatim in
3570	  % the SOURCE QUOTE PROOF of thm:flat_base_change_pushforward below).
3571	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "since $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent ..., it is the
3572	  % quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_S$-module associated to the $A$-module
3573	  % $H^0(S, R^if_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})$ ... Since pullback by $g$
3574	  % corresponds to $- \otimes_A B$ on modules ... we see that it suffices to
3575	  % prove (2)."
3576	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the reduction
3577	  ``part (1) follows from part (2)''.}
3578	  In the cartesian square with \(g\) flat and \(f\) quasi-compact and
3579	  quasi-separated, the sheaf-level base-change map
3580	  \(g^*(f_*\mathcal{F}) \to f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of
3581	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism if and only if,
3582	  after reducing to the affine bases \(S = \operatorname{Spec} A\),
3583	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec} B\), the module comparison map
3584	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) is an
3585	  isomorphism.
3586	  \begin{proof}
3587	    \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
3588	    Being an isomorphism is local on \(S'\), so we may take \(S = \operatorname{Spec} A\)
3589	    and \(S' = \operatorname{Spec} B\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
3590	    quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on \(S\)
3591	    (Stacks Tag 01XJ / Mathlib's quasi-coherence of the pushforward under a
3592	    quasi-compact quasi-separated morphism), hence is the sheaf associated to the
3593	    \(A\)-module \(\Gamma(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})\); likewise
3594	    \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is associated to \(\Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\). Pullback along
3595	    \(g\) corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, so under the
3596	    tilde-equivalence the sheaf base-change map is the tilde of the comparison map
3597	    \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to \Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\); since
3598	    \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is fully faithful on quasi-coherent modules, one is an
3599	    isomorphism iff the other is.
3600	  \end{proof}
3601	\end{lemma}

3616	  % \xymatrix{
3617	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\

3619	  % }
3620	  % $$
3621	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
3622	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
3623	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.

3626	  % \item the base change map of

3628	  % is an isomorphism
3629	  % $$
3630	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
3631	  % $$
3632	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then

3635	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
3636	  \(i = 0\) case.}
3637	  Consider the cartesian square
3638	  \[
3639	    \begin{array}{ccc}
3640	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
3641	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
3642	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
3643	    \end{array}
3644	  \]
3645	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
3646	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
3647	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
3648	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
3649	  \[
3650	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
3651	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
3652	  \]
3653	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
3654	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
3655	  \[
3656	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
3657	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
3658	  \]
3659	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
3660	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
3661	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
3662	
3663	  The hypothesis that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent is essential: it is what
3664	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of
3665	  a module, and it is what lets the affine reduction
3666	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) write \(\mathcal{F}\) as
3667	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be
3668	  an isomorphism.

3741	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
3742	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
3743	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
3744	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
3745	  sequence. The proof is the composite of the decomposition chain.

3747	  \medskip
3748	  \emph{Reduction to the global-sections comparison.} By
3749	  Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_reduce_global_sections}, the base-change map of
3750	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism if and only if,
3751	  after reducing to affine bases \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
3752	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the module comparison map
3753	  \[
3754	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
3755	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
3756	  \]
3757	  is an isomorphism (this uses that \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent — \(f\) is
3758	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated — so each side is the tilde of its module of
3759	  global sections, and that pullback along \(g\) is \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules).
3760	
3761	  \medskip
3762	  \emph{The comparison map is an isomorphism.} Since \(f\) is quasi-compact and
3763	  quasi-separated, \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated over \(A\), so
3764	  Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_mayer_vietoris} applies and gives
3765	  \[
3766	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
3767	  \]
3768	  Internally that lemma presents \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})\) as the finite
3769	  sheaf-condition equalizer of a finite affine cover
3770	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}), base changes the equalizer diagram term by
3771	  term via the affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
3772	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram}), and commutes \(- \otimes_A B\)
3773	  past that finite equalizer by flatness
3774	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}), reducing the general
3775	  quasi-separated case to the separated case
3776	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_separated}) by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the
3777	  number of cover members. Combined with the reduction above, the sheaf-level
3778	  base-change map is an isomorphism.
3779	\end{proof}

950	\end{lemma}
951	
952	\begin{lemma}[The polynomial ring in finitely many variables is Noetherian]
953	  \label{lem:mvPolynomial_isNoetherianRing_fin_mathlib}
954	  \lean{MvPolynomial.isNoetherianRing_fin}
955	  \mathlibok
956	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
957	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.Basic}).}
958	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian commutative ring and \(r \in \mathbb{N}\). Then the
959	  multivariate polynomial ring \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, R)\)
960	  is a Noetherian ring (Hilbert's basis theorem in finitely many variables). With
961	  \(R = \kappa\) a field this makes \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) Noetherian, the
962	  base ring over which the subquotient finiteness is measured
963	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}).
964	\end{lemma}
965	
966	\begin{lemma}[A finite module over a Noetherian ring is Noetherian]
967	  \label{lem:isNoetherian_of_isNoetherianRing_of_finite_mathlib}
968	  \lean{isNoetherian_of_isNoetherianRing_of_finite}
969	  \mathlibok
970	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Noetherian.Defs}).}
971	  Let \(A\) be a Noetherian ring and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then \(M\) is a
972	  Noetherian \(A\)-module. Combined with
973	  \cref{lem:mvPolynomial_isNoetherianRing_fin_mathlib} this makes a finite
974	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module Noetherian, so its submodules are again
975	  finite (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}).
976	\end{lemma}

978	\subsubsection*{The internal grading of a homogeneous submodule (split $G_1$)}
979	
980	\begin{lemma}\leanok
981	[$G_1$ (independence) -- the family $n \mapsto M_n \cap p$ is independent]
982	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}
983	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_inf_iSupIndep}
984	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
985	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and let
986	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
987	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
988	  \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces of \(M\) is independent: for each
989	  \(n\),
990	  \[
991	    (M_n \cap p) \;\cap\; \Bigl(\bigsqcup_{m \ne n} (M_m \cap p)\Bigr) \;=\; 0 .
992	  \]
993	  The whole statement lives in the ambient module \(M\): the subspaces
994	  \(M_n \cap p\) are submodules of \(M\), never of a derived carrier \(\smash{\hat p}\).
995	\end{lemma}
996	
997	\begin{proof}
998	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
999	  \leanok
1000	  The components \(M_n\) of the internal decomposition of \(M\) are independent. The
1001	  subfamily \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) consists of submodules with \(M_n \cap p \le M_n\),
1002	  so independence is inherited: a relation among the \(M_n \cap p\) is in particular a
1003	  relation among the \(M_n\), forcing each term to vanish.
1004	\end{proof}
1005	
1006	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1007	[$G_1$ (supremum) -- $\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) = p$ for $p$ homogeneous]
1008	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
1009	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_inf_eq}
1010	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1011	  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1012	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then
1013	  \[
1014	    \bigsqcup_{n} (M_n \cap p) \;=\; p .
1015	  \]
1016	  Together with the independence of
1017	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}, this is the ambient internal
1018	  direct sum \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\), stated entirely inside \(M\) without
1019	  requiring a direct-sum decomposition of the subtype \(\smash{\hat p}\).
1020	\end{lemma}
1021	
1022	\begin{proof}
1023	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1024	  \leanok
1025	  The inclusion \(\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) \le p\) is clear, since each
1026	  \(M_n \cap p \le p\). For the reverse inclusion, take \(m \in p\) and write
1027	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) using the
1028	  internal decomposition of \(M\). Homogeneity of \(p\) gives
1029	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\), hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n \cap p\); thus
1030	  \(m\) is a finite sum of elements of the \(M_n \cap p\), i.e.
1031	  \(m \in \bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p)\).
1032	\end{proof}
1033	
1034	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient class and its Hilbert function}
1035	
1036	\begin{definition}\leanok
1037	[Subquotient Hilbert data]
1038	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}
1039	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotientHilb}
1040	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum}
1041	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.hilb}
1042	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1043	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
1044	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e.\ \(M\) together with an
1045	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
1046	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
1047	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
1048	  \begin{itemize}
1049	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
1050	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
1051	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
1052	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
1053	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
1054	    \item a finiteness condition: the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a finite module over the
1055	      polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\) (via the
1056	      ambient \(t\)-stable carriers \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N\),
1057	      \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N'\) of \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}).
1058	  \end{itemize}
1059	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
1060	  Hilbert function} is
1061	  \[
1062	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1063	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
1064	  \]
1065	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
1066	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
1067	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
1068	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
1069	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
1070	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
1071	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
1072	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
1073	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
1074	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
1075	\end{definition}
1076	
1077	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
1078	
1079	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
1080	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
1081	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
1082	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
1083	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
1084	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1085	
1086	\begin{definition}\leanok
1087	[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1088	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1089	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1090	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1091	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1092	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1093	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1094	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1095	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1096	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1097	\end{definition}
1098	
1099	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1100	[Membership form of degree-raising]
1101	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1102	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1103	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1104	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1105	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1106	\end{lemma}
1107	
1108	\begin{proof}
1109	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1110	  \leanok
1111	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1112	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1113	\end{proof}
1114	
1115	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1116	[Degree-shift commutation]
1117	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1119	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1120	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1121	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1122	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1123	  \[
1124	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1125	  \]
1126	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1127	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1128	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1129	\end{lemma}
1130	
1131	\begin{proof}
1132	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1133	  \leanok
1134	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous
1135	  components and apply \(x\): \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with
1136	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1137	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1138	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1139	\end{proof}
1140	
1141	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1142	[Degree-raising kills the bottom]
1143	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1145	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1146	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then the degree-\(0\) component of \(x m\) vanishes
1147	  for every \(m\): \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0\).
1148	\end{lemma}
1149	
1150	\begin{proof}
1151	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1152	  \leanok
1153	  Writing \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with each summand in
1154	  \(\mathcal{M}_{j+1}\), no summand has degree \(0\) (every \(j+1 \ge 1\)), so the
1155	  degree-\(0\) component is \(0\).
1156	\end{proof}
1157	
1158	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1159	[Preimage of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1160	  \label{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1161	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_isHomogeneous}
1162	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1163	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N' \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1164	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the preimage \(x^{-1}(N') =
1165	  \{m : x m \in N'\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of \(M\).
1166	\end{lemma}
1167	
1168	\begin{proof}
1169	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1170	  \leanok
1171	  Let \(m \in x^{-1}(N')\) and fix a degree \(i\). By the degree-shift commutation
1172	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}), \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)) =
1173	  \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m)\), which lies in \(N'\) because \(x m \in N'\) and \(N'\)
1174	  is homogeneous. Hence \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\), so \(x^{-1}(N')\) is
1175	  homogeneous.
1176	\end{proof}
1177	
1178	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1179	[Image of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1180	  \label{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1181	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_isHomogeneous}
1182	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1183	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1184	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule.
1185	  Then the image \(x \cdot N = \{x m : m \in N\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of
1186	  \(M\), with degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(x(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) and zero
1187	  degree-\(0\) piece.
1188	\end{lemma}
1189	
1190	\begin{proof}
1191	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1192	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1193	    \leanok
1194	  Take \(x m \in x \cdot N\) with \(m \in N\) and fix a degree. In degree \(0\) the
1195	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0 \in x \cdot N\)
1196	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}). In degree \(i+1\),
1197	  \(\mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\)
1198	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); since \(N\) is homogeneous,
1199	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in N\), so this component lies in \(x \cdot N\). Thus every
1200	  homogeneous component of \(x m\) lies in \(x \cdot N\), which is therefore
1201	  homogeneous.
1202	\end{proof}
1203	
1204	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1205	[Intersection of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1206	  \label{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}
1207	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.inf_isHomogeneous}
1208	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1209	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules
1210	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) then so is their intersection \(p
1211	  \cap q\). Mathlib provides no lattice-closure lemmas for homogeneous submodules, so
1212	  this is recorded here.
1213	\end{lemma}
1214	
1215	\begin{proof}
1216	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1217	  \leanok
1218	  For \(m \in p \cap q\) and any degree \(i\), homogeneity of \(p\) and of \(q\)
1219	  gives \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in q\), hence
1220	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p \cap q\).
1221	\end{proof}
1222	
1223	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1224	[Sum of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1225	  \label{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1226	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_isHomogeneous}
1227	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1228	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules then so is their sum \(p + q\).
1229	\end{lemma}
1230	
1231	\begin{proof}
1232	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1233	  \leanok
1234	  Write an element of \(p + q\) as \(a + b\) with \(a \in p\), \(b \in q\). For any
1235	  degree \(i\), \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) = \mathrm{proj}_i(a) + \mathrm{proj}_i(b)\)
1236	  with \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(b) \in q\) by homogeneity,
1237	  so \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) \in p + q\).
1238	\end{proof}
1239	
1240	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1241	[Ambient image identity]
1242	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1243	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1244	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1245	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1246	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1247	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1248	  \[
1249	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1250	  \]
1251	\end{lemma}
1252	
1253	\begin{proof}
1254	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1255	  \leanok
1256	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1257	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1258	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1259	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1260	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1261	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1262	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1263	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1264	\end{proof}
1265	
1266	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1267	[Ambient distributive law]
1268	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1269	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1270	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1271	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1272	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1273	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1274	  \[
1275	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1276	  \]
1277	\end{lemma}
1278	
1279	\begin{proof}
1280	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1281	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1282	    \leanok
1283	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1284	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1285	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1286	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1287	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1288	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1289	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1290	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1291	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1292	\end{proof}
1293	
1294	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1295	
1296	The kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class is realised by eight ambient
1297	component facts: for a degree-raising endomorphism \(x\) and a homogeneous pair
1298	\(N' \le N\), the kernel pair is \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel pair is
1299	\((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\). The lemmas below record that both new pairs are homogeneous,
1300	nest correctly, are annihilated by \(x\), and are preserved by any endomorphism \(t\)
1301	commuting with \(x\) that preserves the original pair.
1302	
1303	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1304	[Homogeneity of the kernel pair's lower module]
1305	  \label{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}
1306	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_isHomogeneous}
1307	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1308	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and let \(N, N'\) be
1309	  homogeneous submodules of \(M\). Then the kernel pair's lower module
1310	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous. Indeed it is the intersection
1311	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous \(N\) with the homogeneous
1312	  preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}).
1313	\end{lemma}
1314	
1315	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1316	[Homogeneity of the cokernel pair's upper module]
1317	  \label{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}
1318	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_isHomogeneous}
1319	  \uses{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1320	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1321	  homogeneous: it is the sum (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous
1322	  \(N'\) with the homogeneous image \(x \cdot N\)
1323	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}).
1324	\end{lemma}
1325	
1326	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1327	[The kernel pair nests]
1328	  \label{lem:graded_ker_le}
1329	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_le}
1330	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N'\) (\(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)), then
1331	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed \(N' \le N\) by hypothesis, and \(x \cdot N'
1332	  \subseteq N'\) is exactly \(N' \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\); so \(N'\) is below the
1333	  intersection. Hence the kernel pair \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) is a valid
1334	  subquotient pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1335	\end{lemma}
1336	
1337	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1338	[The cokernel pair nests]
1339	  \label{lem:graded_coker_le}
1340	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_le}
1341	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N\) (\(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)), then
1342	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\): both summands lie in \(N\). Hence the cokernel pair
1343	  \((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) is a valid subquotient pair
1344	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1345	\end{lemma}
1346	
1347	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1348	[$x$ annihilates the kernel subquotient]
1349	  \label{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}
1350	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_annihilate}
1351	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries the kernel pair's lower module into \(N'\):
1352	  \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\). Indeed \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq
1353	  x^{-1}(N')\), and applying \(x\) to a preimage of \(N'\) lands in \(N'\). Thus \(x\)
1354	  acts as zero on the kernel subquotient.
1355	\end{lemma}
1356	
1357	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1358	[$x$ annihilates the cokernel subquotient]
1359	  \label{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}
1360	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_annihilate}
1361	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries \(N\) into the cokernel pair's upper module:
1362	  \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\), trivially (the right summand). Thus \(x\)
1363	  acts as zero on the cokernel subquotient \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\).
1364	\end{lemma}
1365	
1366	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1367	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the preimage]
1368	  \label{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_map_le_of_commute}
1370	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N'\) (\(t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)). Then
1371	  \(t\) preserves the preimage: \(t \cdot x^{-1}(N') \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed for
1372	  \(m\) with \(x m \in N'\) one has \(x(t m) = t(x m) \in t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\), so
1373	  \(t m \in x^{-1}(N')\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the kernel
1374	  pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\).
1375	\end{lemma}
1376	
1377	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1378	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the image]
1379	  \label{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1380	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_map_le_of_commute}
1381	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N\) (\(t \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then
1382	  \(t\) preserves the image: \(t \cdot (x \cdot N) \subseteq x \cdot N\). Indeed for
1383	  \(m \in N\) one has \(t(x m) = x(t m)\) with \(t m \in N\), so \(t(x m) \in x \cdot
1384	  N\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the cokernel pair's upper
1385	  module \(N' + x \cdot N\).
1386	\end{lemma}
1387	
1388	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1389	[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1390	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1391	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.ker}
1392	  % This is a narrative grouping lemma: its content is realised in Lean by the eight
1393	  % ambient component facts cited in \uses{} below (no single Lean declaration),
1394	  % together with the SubquotientDatum.ker/.coker constructors that bundle them into a
1395	  % length-(r-1) datum. The `\lean` pin names the `.ker` constructor — the single decl most
1396	  % representative of the bundled closure — so this dependency-bearing grouping node (which is
1397	  % `\uses`'d by the keystone rationality induction) stays wired into the graph rather than
1398	  % orphaning the component facts it `\uses`. This is a deliberate duplicate of the
1399	  % `def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker` pin.
1400	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1401	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1402	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1403	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1404	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1405	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1406	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1407	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1408	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1409	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1410	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1411	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1412	  \[
1413	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1414	    \qquad
1415	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1416	  \]
1417	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1418	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1419	  \begin{enumerate}
1420	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1421	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1422	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1423	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1424	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1425	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1426	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1427	  \end{enumerate}
1428	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1429	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1430	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1431	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1432	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1433	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1434	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1435	\end{lemma}
1436	
1437	\begin{proof}
1438	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1439	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1440	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1441	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1442	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1443	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1444	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1445	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1446	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1447	    \leanok
1448	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1449	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1450	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1451	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1452	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1453	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1454	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1455	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1456	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1457	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1458	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1459	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1460	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1461	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1462	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1463	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1464	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1465	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1466	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1467	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1468	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1469	
1470	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1471	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1472	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1473	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1474	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1475	
1476	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1477	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1478	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1479	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1480	
1481	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1482	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1483	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1484	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1485	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1486	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1487	\end{proof}
1488	
1489	\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}
1490	
1491	\begin{lemma}
1492	
1493	\leanok
1494	[preimage dimension equals ambient-intersection dimension]
1495	  \label{lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype}
1496	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finrank_comap_subtype}
1497	  For submodules \(p, S\) of a \(\kappa\)-vector space \(M\), the dimension of the
1498	  preimage of \(S\) under the inclusion \(p \hookrightarrow M\) equals the dimension of
1499	  the ambient intersection: \(\dim_\kappa(\iota_p^{-1} S) = \dim_\kappa(p \cap S)\),
1500	  where \(\iota_p : p \to M\) is the canonical injection. Project-local helper consumed
1501	  by the two kernel-dimension computations of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}.
1502	\end{lemma}
1503	\begin{proof}
1504	
1505	\leanok
1506	  The inclusion \(\iota_p\) is injective, so it restricts to a \(\kappa\)-linear
1507	  isomorphism from \(\iota_p^{-1} S\) onto its image \(\iota_p(\iota_p^{-1} S) = p \cap S\)
1508	  (image of a comap under an injective map). An isomorphism preserves dimension, giving
1509	  the claimed equality.
1510	\end{proof}
1511	
1512	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1513	[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
1514	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1516	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1517	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1518	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype,
1519	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1520	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1521	  % NOTE: The proof factors the two kernel-dimension computations through the
1522	  %   helper \cref{lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype} (preimage dimension = ambient
1523	  %   intersection dimension).
1524	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1525	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1526	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1527	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1528	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1529	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1530	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1531	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1532	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1533	  \[
1534	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1535	  \]
1536	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1537	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1538	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1539	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1540	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1541	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1542	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1543	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1544	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1545	\end{lemma}
1546	
1547	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1548	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1549	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1550	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1551	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1552	%  following short exact sequence
1553	%  $$
1554	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1555	%  $$
1556	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1557	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1558	\begin{proof}
1559	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1560	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1561	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1562	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1563	    \leanok
1564	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1565	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1566	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1567	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1568	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1569	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1570	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1571	
1572	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1573	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1574	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1575	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1576	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1577	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1578	
1579	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1580	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1581	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1582	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1583	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1584	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1585	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1586	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1587	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1588	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1589	
1590	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1591	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1592	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1593	  \[
1594	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1595	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1596	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1597	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1598	  \]
1599	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1600	\end{proof}
1601	
1602	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1603	
1604	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1605	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1606	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1607	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1608	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1609	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1610	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1611	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1612	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1613	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1614	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
1615	
1616	\begin{definition}\leanok
1617	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1618	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1619	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1620	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1621	  Let \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) be a family of
1622	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1623	  ring homomorphism}
1624	  \[
1625	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \longrightarrow
1626	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)
1627	  \]
1628	  sends each variable \(X_i\) to \(t_i\). It is constructed by evaluating into the
1629	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1630	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1631	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1632	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1633	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
1634	  homomorphism, which is why the commutative \(A\) is interposed.
1635	\end{definition}
1636	
1637	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1638	[Evaluation sends $X_i$ to $t_i$]
1639	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1640	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_X}
1641	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1642	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) for each \(i\). Immediate from the construction
1643	  (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}), as evaluation sends \(X_i\) to the chosen image
1644	  \(t_i\).
1645	\end{lemma}
1646	
1647	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1648	[Evaluation sends a constant to a scalar]
1649	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1650	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_C}
1651	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1652	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\) for each \(c \in \kappa\):
1653	  the evaluation homomorphism (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}) is \(\kappa\)-linear and

1730	\subsubsection*{Finiteness-transfer infrastructure}
1731	
1732	The ambient finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer})
1733	factors the polynomial action of the larger ring through a surjection onto the
1734	smaller one. The following blocks build that surjection and the semilinearity
1735	identity it rests on, and record the two elementary finiteness manipulations
1736	(shrinking a numerator, enlarging a denominator) used to feed the kernel and
1737	cokernel constructors.

1739	\begin{definition}

1741	\leanok
1742	[The last-variable surjection of polynomial rings]
1743	  \label{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}

1745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_castSucc}

1749	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_surjective}
1750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_ringHomSurjective}
1751	  \uses{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1752	  For a field \(\kappa\) and \(r \in \mathbb{N}\), the \emph{last-variable
1753	  homomorphism} is the \(\kappa\)-algebra map
1754	  \[
1755	    \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1756	      = \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r\}, \kappa)
1757	      \;\longrightarrow\;
1758	      \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r-1\}, \kappa) = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1759	  \]
1760	  obtained by evaluation: it sends the last variable \(X_r\) to \(0\) and each
1761	  earlier variable \(X_i\) (for \(0 \le i < r\)) to \(X_i\), and fixes constants.
1762	  It is a left inverse of the algebra map
1763	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1764	  induced by the canonical inclusion \(\{0, \dots, r-1\} \hookrightarrow \{0, \dots, r\}\)
1765	  on variable indices, and is therefore surjective. This is the concrete
1766	  ring surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1767	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) along which finiteness is transferred
1768	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
1769	\end{definition}

1803	      \;-\; \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})
