1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	
47	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
48	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
49	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
50	  % NOTE (iter-022): CLOSED, axiom-clean {propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}.
51	  % The Lean decl is single-universe `(A B M : Type u)` (was `Type*`): L5 and
52	  % `free_localizationAway_of_away_tower` are single-universe, and the dévissage
53	  % applies them to `C = B ⧸ 𝔭`, forcing A, B into one universe. Faithful for the
54	  % geometric use (scheme patches are single-universe).
55	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
56	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
57	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
58	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
59	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
60	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
61	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
62	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
63	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
64	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
65	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
66	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
67	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
68	
69	\end{theorem}
70	
71	% INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold pass creates the decl pinned above with
72	% exactly this signature):
73	%   (A B M : Type u) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]   % single-universe (iter-022)
74	%     [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra.FiniteType A B]
75	%     [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
76	%     [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
77	%     ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
78	%       Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M)
79	
80	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
81	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
82	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
83	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
84	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
85	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
86	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
87	% there exists a finite filtration
88	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
89	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
90	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
91	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
92	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
93	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
94	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
95	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
96	\begin{proof}
97	
98	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
99	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
100	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
101	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
102	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
103	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
104	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
105	
106	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
107	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
108	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
109	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
110	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
111	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
112	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
113	  for exactly this residue.
114	
115	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
116	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
117	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
118	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
119	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
120	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
121	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
122	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
123	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
124	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
125	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
126	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
127	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
128	\end{proof}
129	
130	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
131	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
132	
133	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
134	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
135	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
136	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
137	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
138	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
139	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
140	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
141	
142	\begin{lemma}[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
143	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
145	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
146	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
147	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
148	\end{lemma}
149	
150	\begin{proof}
151	
152	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
153	  finitely presented, so
154	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
155	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
156	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
157	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
158	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
159	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
160	  \(f := r\).
161	\end{proof}
162	
163	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
164	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
165	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
166	  \mathlibok
167	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
168	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
169	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
170	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
171	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r\) (of the same finite rank).
172	\end{lemma}
173	
174	\begin{lemma}[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
175	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
176	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
177	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
178	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
179	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
180	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
181	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
182	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
183	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
184	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
185	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
186	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
187	  induction).}
188	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
189	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
190	  \(A_f\).
191	\end{lemma}
192	
193	\begin{proof}
194	
195	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
196	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
197	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
198	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
199	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
200	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
201	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
202	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
203	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
204	\end{proof}
205	
206	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
207	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
208	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
209	  \mathlibok
210	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
211	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
212	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
213	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
214	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
215	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
216	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
217	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
218	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
219	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
220	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
221	\end{lemma}
222	
223	\begin{lemma}[L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact]
224	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}
225	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact}
226	  Let \(0 \to M' \xrightarrow{i} M \xrightarrow{q} M'' \to 0\) be a short exact
227	  sequence of \(B\)-modules, and let \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). Restricting scalars
228	  along \(A \to B\) and localising every term at the powers of \(f\) yields a short
229	  exact sequence
230	  \[
231	    0 \to M'_f \xrightarrow{i_f} M_f \xrightarrow{q_f} M''_f \to 0
232	  \]
233	  of \(A_f\)-modules; in particular \(i_f\) is injective and \(q_f\) is surjective.
234	\end{lemma}
235	
236	\begin{proof}
237	
238	  Localisation at a multiplicative set is an exact functor, preserving injections,
239	  surjections, and exactness. Applying it to the scalar-restricted \(A\)-linear maps
240	  \(i\) and \(q\) (via \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence.
241	\end{proof}
242	
243	\begin{lemma}[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
244	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
245	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
246	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
247	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
248	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
249	\end{lemma}
250	
251	\begin{proof}
252	
253	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
254	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
255	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
256	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
257	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
258	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
259	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
260	\end{proof}
261	
262	\begin{lemma}[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
263	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
264	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
265	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
266	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
267	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
268	\end{lemma}
269	
270	\begin{proof}
271	
272	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
273	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
274	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
275	\end{proof}
276	
277	\begin{lemma}[L3 — splicing fact for a short exact sequence (assembly)]
278	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}
279	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_shortExact}
280	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
281	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
282	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark
283	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1737-L1745).
284	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
285	  % "Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of
286	  %  $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of $A$ such that
287	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,
288	  %  then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$ where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact
289	  %  repeatedly. Therefore it is enough to prove the result when $M$ is of the form
290	  %  $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. This reduces us to the case where $B$
291	  %  is a domain and $M=B$."
292	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (splicing fact).}
293	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
294	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and
295	  \(M''_{f''}\) free over \(A_{f''}\). Then for \(f := f' f''\) the localisation
296	  \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
297	\end{lemma}
298	
299	\begin{proof}
300	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
301	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
302	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).
303	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (splicing fact).}
304	  Set \(f := f' f''\); since \(A\) is a domain and \(f', f'' \ne 0\), the product is
305	  non-zero, so \(f \ne 0\). Localising the given short exact sequence at the powers
306	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence
307	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules, by
308	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}. Both ends are free over \(A_f\):
309	  applying \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport} to \(M'\) with the
310	  factorisation \(f = f' f''\) (using that \(M'_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\)) shows
311	  \(M'_f\) is free over \(A_f\), and applying the same lemma to \(M''\) with the
312	  factorisation \(f = f'' f'\) (using that \(M''_{f''}\) is free over \(A_{f''}\))
313	  shows \(M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Finally
314	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split} concludes that the middle term \(M_f\) is
315	  free over \(A_f\).
316	\end{proof}

318	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
319	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
320	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
321	  \mathlibok
322	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
323	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
324	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
325	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module
326	  over a polynomial subalgebra on algebraically independent generators.
327	\end{lemma}
328	
329	\begin{lemma}
330	
331	[L4a — clearing one equation's denominators]
332	  \label{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
333	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_clear_one_denominator}
334	  % NOTE (shared engine): the denominator-clearing performed here -- pushing finitely
335	  % many coefficients of one polynomial from K = Frac(A) down into a single
336	  % Localization.Away g -- is the same primitive the torsion reindex (L5b,
337	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex) uses when it inverts the leading coefficient of the
338	  % Nagata-normalised annihilator. A prover who writes one general "clear finitely many
339	  % denominators into one Localization.Away g" helper can have both lemmas consume it.
340	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
341	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).
342	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
343	  % "If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for
344	  %  coefficients of equations of integral dependence
345	  %  satisfied by a finite set of
346	  %  algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$,
347	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
348	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
349	  %   Let K := FractionRing A and A_g := Localization.Away g; the canonical map
350	  %   A_g -> K (induced because powers of g are non-zero-divisors of A) extends along
351	  %   MvPolynomial.map to MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g -> MvPolynomial (Fin n) K.
352	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
353	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (FractionRing A)) :
354	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0),
355	  %         p ∈ Set.range
356	  %           (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)))
357	  % NOTE (encoding): there is NO canonical `Algebra (Localization.Away g)
358	  %   (FractionRing A)` instance, so the landed Lean does not use the literal
359	  %   `algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)` above. It encodes the same map
360	  %   as `IsLocalization.map (FractionRing A) (RingHom.id A) hle` with
361	  %   `hle : powers g ≤ comap id (nonZeroDivisors A)` — mathematically identical, just the
362	  %   well-typed form. The proof is axiom-clean.
363	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
364	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
365	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
366	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
367	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
368	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
369	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
370	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
371	\end{lemma}
372	
373	\begin{proof}
374	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
375	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
376	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
377	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
378	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
379	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
380	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
381	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
382	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
383	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
384	\end{proof}

386	\begin{lemma}[L4 — Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators]
387	  \label{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}
388	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial}
389	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator,
390	        lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
391	  % NOTE: the LEAN SIGNATURE block below now matches the landed
392	  % Lean decl `exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial`, which RETAINS the binder
393	  % `(_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))` as
394	  % the 3rd existential (after `(_ : g ≠ 0)`, before `φ`) — required to type the
395	  % AlgHom target as an A_g-algebra. The MATH statement is unaffected.
396	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (landed target, AlgHom encoding). Write
397	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g,   B_g := Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g).
398	  % The conclusion is:
399	  %   ∃ (n : ℕ) (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
400	  %     (_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))
401	  %     (φ : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g)
402	  %           →ₐ[Localization.Away g] Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)),
403	  %     Function.Injective φ ∧
404	  %     (letI := φ.toAlgebra;
405	  %      Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g))
406	  %                    (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g))) ∧
407	  %     (∀ a, algebraMap A_g B_g (algebraMap A A_g a)
408	  %             = algebraMap B B_g (algebraMap A B a))
409	  % NOTE (iter-022): a FOURTH existential conjunct was added — the chosen
410	  % `algBg : Algebra A_g B_g` is COMPATIBLE with the canonical `A → B → B_g`
411	  % tower. Without it the existential's `Algebra A_g B_g` is arbitrary and the
412	  % downstream `genericFlatnessAlgebraic` step-4 bridge cannot prove
413	  % `IsScalarTower A A_g C_g`. The MATH statement is unaffected (the compatible
414	  % structure is the `awayMap` one); the conjunct just pins it across the ∃.
415	  % The polynomial subalgebra A_g[b_1,…,b_n] is realised as the DOMAIN
416	  % `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`, embedded into B_g by the algebra map φ; the
417	  % generators b_j are φ(X_j). Compared with an earlier draft using three anonymous
418	  % instance existentials (∃ (_ : Algebra A_g B_g) (_ : Algebra (MvPoly … A_g) B_g)
419	  % (_ : IsScalarTower …)), this form keeps only the single `Algebra A_g B_g` binder
420	  % needed to type B_g as an A_g-algebra: the single φ then carries the algebra map,
421	  % `Function.Injective φ` encodes algebraic independence of the b_j, and
422	  % `Module.Finite … over φ.toAlgebra` encodes module-finiteness of B_g over the
423	  % image polynomial subalgebra. Downstream, L5 (\cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core})
424	  % consumes a finite module over `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`; the AlgHom hands it
425	  % exactly that algebra structure via `φ.toAlgebra`, with no instance revival.
426	  % Why B_g and not B: generic flatness only needs the property after one
427	  % inversion g; the integral-dependence equations only clear denominators after
428	  % inverting g (see the SOURCE QUOTE PROOF below).
429	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
430	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
431	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
432	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
433	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
434	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
435	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
436	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
437	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
438	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
439	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
440	  normalisation with denominators).}
441	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
442	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
443	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
444	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
445	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
446	  \[
447	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
448	  \]
449	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
450	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
451	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
452	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
453	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
454	\end{lemma}
455	
456	\begin{proof}
457	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
458	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
459	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
460	  % NOTE: the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a Finset-fold over the finite
461	  % generating set sigma, but each fold step is the Mathlib collapsing lemma
462	  % IsIntegral.exists_multiple_integral_of_isLocalization applied to the generator AS A
463	  % WHOLE (not to its individual coefficient polynomials via gf_clear_one_denominator).
464	  % For each generator x, that lemma yields a single a_x in A^0 (nonzero, since A is a
465	  % domain) with C(a_x) * (1 (x) x) integral over MvPolynomial A; set g1 := prod_x a_x and
466	  % g := g0 * g1. The AlgHom assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
467	
468	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
469	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
470	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
471	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
472	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
473	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
474	
475	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
476	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
477	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
478	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
479	  \(x \in \sigma\) apply the Mathlib collapsing lemma
480	  \texttt{IsIntegral.exists\_multiple\_integral\_of\_isLocalization} (with
481	  \(R = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,A\), \(R_m = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,K\), \(S = B_K\), and the localizing
482	  submonoid the image of \(A^{0}\)): this returns a single
483	  \(a_x \in A^{0}\) (non-zero, as \(A\) is a domain) such that \(C(a_x)\cdot(1\otimes x)\)
484	  is integral over \(\mathrm{MvPoly}\,A = A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
485	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
486	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
487	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
488	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
489	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
490	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the landed proof discharges a whole generator
491	  at once via the collapsing lemma, which folds that primitive over the coefficients
492	  internally.)
493	
494	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
495	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
496	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
497	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
498	
499	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
500	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
501	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
502	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
503	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
504	  \[
505	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
506	  \]
507	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
508	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
509	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
510	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
511	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
512	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
513	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
514	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
515	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
516	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
517	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
518	
519	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
520	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
521	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
522	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
523	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
524	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
525	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
526	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
527	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
528	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
529	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
530	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
531	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
532	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
533	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
534	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
535	
536	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
537	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
538	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
539	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
540	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
541	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
542	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
543	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
544	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
545	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
546	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
547	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
548	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
549	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
550	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
551	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
552	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
553	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
554	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
555	\end{proof}
556	
557	\begin{lemma}[injectivity of a localisation lift]
558	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
559	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
560	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
561	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
562	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
563	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
564	\end{lemma}
565	\begin{proof}
566	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
567	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
568	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
569	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
570	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
571	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
572	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
573	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
574	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
575	\end{proof}
576	
577	\begin{lemma}
578	
579	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
580	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
581	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
582	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
583	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
584	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
585	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
586	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
587	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
588	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
589	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
590	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
591	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
592	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
593	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
594	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
595	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
596	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
597	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
598	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
599	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
600	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
601	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
602	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
603	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
604	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
605	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
606	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
607	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
608	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
609	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
610	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
611	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
612	  \[
613	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
614	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
615	  \]
616	
617	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
618	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
619	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
620	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
621	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
622	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
623	\end{lemma}
624	
625	\begin{proof}
626	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
627	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
628	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
629	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
630	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
631	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
632	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
633	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
634	
635	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
636	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
637	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
638	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
639	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
640	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
641	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
642	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
643	
644	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
645	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
646	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
647	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
648	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
649	\end{proof}

661	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
662	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
663	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
664	  \mathlibok
665	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
666	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
667	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
668	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
669	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
670	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
671	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
672	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
673	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M\) a finite torsion \(R\)-module. Then the
674	  annihilator of \(M\) meets the non-zero-divisors of \(R\): the set
675	  \((\top : \operatorname{Submodule} R\,M).\mathrm{annihilator} \cap R^{\circ}\) is
676	  non-empty, where \(R^{\circ}\) denotes the non-zero-divisors of \(R\).
677	\end{lemma}

679	\begin{lemma}[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
680	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
681	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
682	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
683	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
684	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
685	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
686	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
687	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
688	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
689	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
690	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A. The conclusion is the existence of a
691	  %   non-zero annihilator; the downstream consequence "T is finite over P_d/(F)" is
692	  %   left to the assembly (a surjection P_d ↠ P_d/(F) pushes a finite generating set).
693	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)
694	  %       (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
695	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
696	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
697	  %       ∃ F : MvPolynomial (Fin d) A, F ≠ 0 ∧
698	  %         F ∈ Module.annihilator (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T
699	  %   (F ∈ annihilator unfolds to ∀ x : T, F • x = 0.)
700	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
701	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
702	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
703	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
704	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
705	\end{lemma}
706	
707	\begin{proof}
708	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
709	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
710	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
711	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
712	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
713	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
714	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
715	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
716	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
717	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
718	\end{proof}

720	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
721	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
722	
723	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
724	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
725	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
726	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
727	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
728	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
729	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
730	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
731	
732	\begin{definition}[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
733	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
734	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
735	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
736	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
737	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
738	  \[
739	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
740	  \]
741	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
742	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
743	\end{definition}
744	
745	\begin{lemma}[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
746	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
747	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
748	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
749	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
750	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
751	\end{lemma}
752	\begin{proof}
753	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
754	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
755	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\).
756	\end{proof}
757	
758	\begin{definition}[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
759	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
760	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
761	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
762	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
763	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
764	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
765	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
766	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
767	\end{definition}
768	
769	\begin{lemma}[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
770	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
771	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
772	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
773	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
774	\end{lemma}
775	\begin{proof}
776	  The list of values of \(v\) consists exactly of the entries \(v_i\); each satisfies
777	  \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\) by hypothesis.
778	\end{proof}
779	
780	\begin{lemma}[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
781	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
782	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
783	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
784	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
785	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
786	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
787	\end{lemma}
788	\begin{proof}
789	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
790	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
791	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
792	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
793	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
794	\end{proof}

796	\begin{lemma}[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
797	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
798	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
799	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
800	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
801	  transformed monomial is the radix value
802	  \[
803	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
804	  \]
805	\end{lemma}
806	\begin{proof}
807	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
808	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
809	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
810	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
811	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
812	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
813	\end{proof}
814	
815	\begin{lemma}[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
816	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
817	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
818	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
819	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
820	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
821	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
822	\end{lemma}
823	\begin{proof}
824	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
825	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
826	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
827	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
828	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
829	\end{proof}

831	\begin{lemma}[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
832	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
833	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
834	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
835	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
836	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
837	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
838	\end{lemma}
839	\begin{proof}
840	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
841	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
842	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
843	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
844	\end{proof}
845	
846	\begin{lemma}[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
847	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
848	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
849	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
850	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
851	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
852	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
853	\end{lemma}
854	\begin{proof}
855	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
856	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
857	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
858	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
859	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
860	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
861	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
862	\end{proof}

864	\begin{lemma}[Naturality of the variable-splitting isomorphism under base change]
865	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
866	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
867	  For any ring homomorphism \(\varphi : A \to B\) and polynomial \(q \in A[X_0, \dots, X_m]\),
868	  the isomorphism \(A[X_0, \dots, X_m] \cong (A[X_1, \dots, X_m])[X_0]\) is natural with
869	  respect to applying \(\varphi\) coefficientwise.
870	\end{lemma}
871	\begin{proof}
872	  By induction on \(q\): the isomorphism is additive and multiplicative, and agrees with
873	  \(\varphi\) on constants and variables, so it commutes with \(\varphi\) on all polynomials.
874	\end{proof}

1942	
1943	The kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) and the
1944	abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1945	bundled into two constructors that take a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum to the
1946	length-\(r\) kernel and cokernel data consumed by the induction. The two stability
1947	lemmas below first record that the new pairs are stable under the \emph{whole}
1948	endomorphism family (not merely the reduced one), which is what lets the over-\(\kappa[t_0,\dots,t_r]\)
1949	finiteness inputs be stated before the variable is dropped.

2045	
2046	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2047	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
2048	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
2049	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
2050	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2051	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2052	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
2053	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
2054	  \[
2055	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
2056	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
2057	  \]
2058	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
2059	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
2060	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
2061	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
2062	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
2063	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
2064	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
2065	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
2066	  \[
2067	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
2068	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
2069	  \]
2070	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
2071	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
2072	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
2073	\end{lemma}

2076	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2077	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2078	    \leanok
2079	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
2080	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
2081	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
2082	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
2083	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
2084	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
2085	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
2086	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
2087	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
2088	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
2089	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
2090	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and

2135	  that \(\pi(g_i) \cap \sum_{j \ne i} \pi(g_j) = 0\). Take \(q\) in that intersection;
2136	  write \(q = \pi(a)\) with \(a \in g_i\), and (pushing the sum through \(\pi\), since
2137	  \(\pi(\sum_{j\ne i} g_j) = \sum_{j\ne i}\pi(g_j)\)) \(q = \pi(b)\) with
2138	  \(b \in \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). Then \(a - b \in \ker\pi\), so
2139	  \(a = (a-b) + b \in \ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). By the hypothesis applied at \(i\),
2140	  \(a \in \ker\pi\), whence \(q = \pi(a) = 0\).
2141	\end{proof}
2142	
2143	\begin{lemma}
2144	
2145	\leanok
2146	[Base case: the length-zero subquotient Hilbert function vanishes eventually]
2147	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2148	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2149	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb,
2150	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2151	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep, lem:graded_iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup,
2152	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2153	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2154	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2155	  % 00K1), proof, base case (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex,
2156	  % L13908-L13910).
2157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2158	  % "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2159	  %  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2160	  %  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds."
2161	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (base case of the induction).}
2162	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(0\)
2163	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}): a homogeneous pair \(N' \le N\) of the fixed
2164	  ambient graded module \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) with \emph{no} endomorphisms,
2165	  whose finiteness witness says the subquotient \(Q_0 := N/N'\) is module-finite over
2166	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa)\). Then the ambient Hilbert
2167	  function vanishes eventually: there is \(K \in \mathbb{N}\) with
2168	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for all \(n > K\). Consequently
2169	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\) (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}),
2170	  which is the base case of the ambient subquotient induction
2171	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}).
2172	\end{lemma}
2173	
2174	\begin{proof}
2175	  \uses{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2176	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
2177	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2178	    \leanok
2179	  Since the index set of variables is empty,
2180	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), so the finiteness
2181	  witness makes the subquotient \(Q_0 = N/N'\) a \emph{finite-dimensional}
2182	  \(\kappa\)-vector space
2183	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}). For each
2184	  degree \(n\), composing the inclusion of the homogeneous piece
2185	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \hookrightarrow N\) with the quotient projection
2186	  \(N \twoheadrightarrow Q_0\) gives a \(\kappa\)-linear map
2187	  \(\psi_n : N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \to Q_0\), \(m \mapsto [m]\), whose image is the
2188	  class of the degree-\(n\) homogeneous piece inside the subquotient.
2189	
2190	  \emph{Step 1: independence of the images.} The family
2191	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\bigr)_{n}\) is \(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\) in
2192	  \(Q_0\). The reason is the ambient direct-sum grading of \(M\): the homogeneous
2193	  pieces \(\mathcal{M}_n\) form an independent family
2194	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}). An element of
2195	  \(\bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)\) is a finite sum of classes of
2196	  homogeneous elements of degrees \(j \ne n\); since \(N'\) is homogeneous, the
2197	  degree-\(n\) homogeneous component of any representative of such a class lies in
2198	  \(N'\), so the class has zero degree-\(n\) component in \(Q_0\). A nonzero class in
2199	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\), by contrast, is represented by a homogeneous
2200	  element of degree \(n\) not in \(N'\), so has nonzero degree-\(n\) component.
2201	  Comparing degree-\(n\) components forces the intersection
2202	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \cap \bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)
2203	  = 0\); independence follows.
2204	  % NOTE: ROUTE (b). Prove the iSupIndep by direct membership analysis in
2205	  %   ⨆_{j ≠ n} range(ψ j), reading off the degree-n homogeneous component
2206	  %   directly inside Q₀'s fixed κ-module structure.
2207	  %   No outgoing linear map out of Q₀ is constructed, so no scalar ring other than κ
2208	  %   is ever introduced.
2209	  % NOTE: ROUTE (a). Building a κ-linear detector
2210	  %   Φ : Q₀ → M/N', [m] ↦ [π_n m] (degree-n projection) via descent through the
2211	  %   subquotient fails: the descended map carries the scalar action of Q₀'s base
2212	  %   ring, but the target M/N' carries only a κ-module structure -- a scalar-ring
2213	  %   incompatibility. Route (b) avoids this by working entirely within Q₀.
2214	
2215	  \emph{Step 2: finiteness of the support.} Because \(Q_0\) is finite-dimensional and
2216	  the ranges \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\) are independent, only finitely many of
2217	  them are nonzero: the set \(\{\,n : \operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \ne 0\,\}\) is
2218	  finite (\cref{lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib}). In particular it is bounded
2219	  above, by some \(K \in \mathbb{N}\).
2220	
2221	  \emph{Step 3: eventual vanishing.} For \(n > K\) we have
2222	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) = 0\), i.e.\ every homogeneous element of
2223	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\) has class \(0\) in \(Q_0 = N/N'\); equivalently
2224	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N'\), hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)
2225	  and
2226	  \[
2227	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2228	      - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n) \;=\; 0 .
2229	  \]
2230	  Thus \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero, and
2231	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2232	\end{proof}

2236	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2237	[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
2238	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
2239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
2240	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2241	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2242	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2243	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2244	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2245	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2246	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2247	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2248	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2249	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2250	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
2251	  degree-one generators).}
2252	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
2253	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
2254	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
2255	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2256	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
2257	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
2258	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
2259	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
2260	  \(M\).
2261	\end{lemma}
2262	
2263	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
2264	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2265	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2266	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2267	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
2268	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
2269	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
2270	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
2271	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
2272	\begin{proof}
2273	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2274	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2275	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2276	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2277	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2278	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2279	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2280	    \leanok
2281	  We induct on the length \(r\).
2282	
2283	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} A length-zero datum has a finiteness witness making the
2284	  subquotient \(N/N'\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space
2285	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}); its degreewise
2286	  images form an independent family inside that finite-dimensional space, so only
2287	  finitely many are nonzero and \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for \(n \gg 0\)
2288	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}). By
2289	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is rational of order
2290	  \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2291	
2292	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
2293	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
2294	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
2295	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), assembled by
2296	  the kernel and cokernel constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
2297	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}) whose finiteness witnesses come from the
2298	  abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The
2299	  induction hypothesis applied
2300	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
2301	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
2302	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2303	  \[
2304	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
2305	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
2306	  \]
2307	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
2308	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
2309	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
2310	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
2311	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
2312	\end{proof}

2314	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2315	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2316	
2317	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2318	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2319	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2320	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2321	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2322	
2323	\begin{definition}
2324	
2325	\leanok
2326	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
2327	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
2328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
2329	  % NOTE: The definition carries no \uses: the Lean construction is
2330	  % `IdealSheafData.ofIdeals (fun U => Module.annihilator Γ(X,U) Γ(F,U))`, which is well-defined
2331	  % unconditionally and does NOT depend on the localization bridges at definition time. The two
2332	  % facts lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map and lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen are needed
2333	  % only for the downstream CHARACTERIZATION (the reverse inclusion / equality on affine opens, the
2334	  % "annihilator forward direction"), which is not yet a blueprint block.
2335	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
2336	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
2337	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
2338	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
2339	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
2340	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2341	  % Polynomials" (read from
2342	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2343	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2344	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
2345	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
2346	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2347	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2348	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2349	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
2350	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
2351	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
2352	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
2353	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
2354	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
2355	  \(U \subseteq X\),
2356	  \[
2357	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2358	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
2359	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
2360	  \]
2361	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
2362	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
2363	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2364	
2365	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
2366	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
2367	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
2368	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
2369	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
2370	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
2371	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
2372	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
2373	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
2374	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
2375	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
2376	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2377	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
2378	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
2379	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
2380	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2381	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
2382	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
2383	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
2384	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
2385	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
2386	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
2387	
2388	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
2389	\end{definition}
2390	
2391	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2392	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
2393	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
2394	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
2395	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
2396	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
2397	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
2398	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
2399	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
2400	  \(U\):
2401	  \[
2402	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2403	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
2404	  \]
2405	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
2406	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
2407	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
2408	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
2409	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
2410	\end{lemma}
2411	
2412	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2413	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
2414	  localization]
2415	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
2416	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
2417	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
2418	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
2419	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
2420	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
2421	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
2422	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
2423	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
2424	  \[
2425	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
2426	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
2427	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
2428	  \]
2429	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2430	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2431	
2432	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2433	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2434	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2435	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2436	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2437	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2438	\end{lemma}
2439	
2440	\begin{proof}
2441	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2442	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2443	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2444	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2445	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2446	
2447	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2448	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2449	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2450	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2451	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2452	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2453	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2454	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2455	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2456	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2457	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2458	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2459	\end{proof}
2460	
2461	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
2462	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2463	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
2464	  \mathlibok
2465	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2466	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
2467	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
2468	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
2469	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
2470	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
2471	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
2472	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
2473	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
2474	\end{lemma}
2475	
2476	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
2477	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2478	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
