2494	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
2495	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
2496	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
2497	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2498	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
2499	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
2500	  %  associated to $M$.
2501	  %  [...]
2502	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
2503	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
2504	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
2505	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
2506	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
2507	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
2508	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
2509	  \begin{enumerate}
2510	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
2511	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
2512	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2513	    \item the section-restriction map
2514	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2515	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2516	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2517	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2518	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
2519	  \end{enumerate}
2520	\end{lemma}
2521	
2522	\begin{proof}
2523	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2524	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2525	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2526	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2527	
2528	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
2529	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
2530	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
2531	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
2532	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
2533	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
2534	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
2535	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
2536	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
2537	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
2538	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
2539	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
2540	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
2541	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
2542	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
2543	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
2544	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
2545	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
2546	\end{proof}

2548	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2549	[Basic-open restriction of a $\widetilde{N}$ sheaf is a module localization]
2550	  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
2551	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
2552	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (tag 020J), \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
2553	  % \widetilde M on Spec(R), item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
2554	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2555	  % "For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$ as an $R_f$-module."
2556	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, item (4); Spec-local core of
2557	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}, part (2).}
2558	  Let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module and \(f \in R\). Then the presheaf restriction map of the
2559	  associated sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\) on \(\Spec R\) from the global sections
2560	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, \top)\) to the sections on the basic open \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f))\)
2561	  exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f))\) as the localized module
2562	  \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\); that is, it is \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\)
2563	  over \(R\).
2564	\end{lemma}
2565	\begin{proof}
2566	  Mathlib supplies the localization-out-of-\(N\) instance
2567	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) for the canonical map
2568	  \(N \to \Gamma(\widetilde N, D(f))\). Since the global-sections map
2569	  \(N \to \Gamma(\widetilde N, \top)\) is an isomorphism and the canonical map to \(D(f)\) factors
2570	  through it followed by the restriction, precomposing the localization map with the inverse
2571	  isomorphism transfers the \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\) property to the restriction map
2572	  (Mathlib's transfer lemma for localization along a linear equivalence).
2573	\end{proof}

2575	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2576	[Basic-open restriction localizes for a sheaf in the essential image of $\widetilde{(-)}$]
2577	  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
2578	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
2579	  \uses{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
2580	  \textit{Affine engine of \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}: the basic-open
2581	  restriction localizes for any module sheaf that is \(\widetilde{N}\) up to isomorphism.}
2582	  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) such that the counit
2583	  \(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism (equivalently, \(M\) lies in the essential image of
2584	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\); Mathlib's \(\mathrm{isIso\_fromTildeΓ\_iff}\)), and let \(f \in R\). Then the
2585	  presheaf restriction of \(M\) from \(\Gamma(M, \top)\) to \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\) is
2586	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(R\).
2587	\end{lemma}
2588	\begin{proof}
2589	  The image of \(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) under \(\mathrm{modulesSpecToSheaf}\) is an isomorphism;
2590	  forgetting to the presheaf gives a natural isomorphism \(\psi\) whose components are isomorphisms
2591	  of \(R\)-modules. Its naturality square for the inclusion \(D(f) \hookrightarrow \top\)
2592	  intertwines the restriction maps of \(\widetilde{N}\) (for \(N = \Gamma(M, \top)\)) and of \(M\).
2593	  Transporting \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict} across the two component isomorphisms
2594	  (pre- and post-composing by the linear equivalences induced by the components of \(\psi\))
2595	  identifies the restriction of \(M\) with a localization map, hence it is
2596	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\).

2750	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherence = a cover with local presentations]
2751	  \label{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib}

2756	  A sheaf of modules \(M\) on a scheme is \emph{quasi-coherent}
2757	  (\(\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\,M\)) exactly when the type of \emph{quasi-coherence data}
2758	  \(\mathrm{QuasicoherentData}\,M\) is nonempty. A datum
2759	  \(q : \mathrm{QuasicoherentData}\,M\) consists of an index family \(\{X_i\}\) of opens covering the
2760	  whole scheme together with, for each \(i\), a presentation
2761	  \((M.\mathrm{over}\,X_i).\mathrm{Presentation}\) of the restriction of \(M\) to \(X_i\)
2762	  (a cokernel-of-free-modules description on the site over \(X_i\)). Thus quasi-coherence packages a
2763	  cover on which \(M\) is locally presented; on each member of the cover
2764	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation_mathlib} applies.

2793	\begin{lemma}[Restriction is isomorphic to pullback]
2794	  \label{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}

2798	  For an open immersion \(f\), the restriction functor (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib})
2799	  is naturally isomorphic to the pullback functor (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}):
2800	  \(f^{\mathrm{res}} \cong f^{*}\). This identifies the restriction functor with the
2801	  universal-property pullback, so a presentation transported along one transports along the other.
2802	\end{lemma}

2809	  Let \(F\) be a functor between categories of sheaves of modules that preserves colimits and is
2810	  equipped with an isomorphism \(\eta : F(\mathrm{unit}_R) \cong \mathrm{unit}_S\) sending the unit
2811	  sheaf to the unit sheaf. Then any presentation \(P : \mathrm{Presentation}\,M\) (a
2812	  cokernel-of-free-modules description) induces a presentation
2813	  \(P.\mathrm{map}\,F\,\eta : \mathrm{Presentation}\,(F(M))\) of the image. Applied to the pullback
2814	  and restriction functors (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib},
2815	  \cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}), it is how a presentation of \(M\) over one site is
2816	  carried to a presentation over another.

2839	  If \(S\) is a localization of a commutative ring \(R\) at a multiplicative submonoid, then \(S\) is

2841	  what lets the section-localization functor pass through the finite sheaf equalizer in the descent
2842	  (\cref{lem:section_localization_descent}).

2845	% --- The finite basic-open cover (topological precursor) ---
2846	
2847	\begin{lemma}
2848	
2849	\leanok
2850	[Finite basic-open cover refining a quasi-coherence cover]
2851	  \label{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}
2852	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}
2853	  \uses{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib, lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}
2854	  \textit{The topological finite-cover front of the gap1 transport: any quasi-coherence cover refines
2855	  to a finite basic-open cover.}
2856	  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) and let \(q\) be quasi-coherence data for \(M\)
2857	  (\cref{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib}): a — possibly infinite — open cover
2858	  \(\{q.X_i\}\) of \(\top\) together with a presentation of \(M.\mathrm{over}\,(q.X_i)\) on each
2859	  member. Then there is a \emph{finite} subset \(t \subseteq R\) whose basic opens cover \(\Spec R\)
2860	  (equivalently \(\mathrm{span}\,t = (1)\)) such that each \(D(r)\), \(r \in t\), is contained in some
2861	  member \(q.X_i\) of the presentation cover.
2862	\end{lemma}
2863	\begin{proof}
2864	  \uses{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}
2865	  The set \(G = \{\,r \in R : D(r) \le q.X_i \text{ for some } i\,\}\) spans the unit ideal. Indeed
2866	  every prime of \(R\) lies in some member \(q.X_i\) by the covering property \(q.\mathrm{coversTop}\)
2867	  (read off the \(\mathrm{Opens}\) Grothendieck-topology sieve), and the basic opens form a basis of
2868	  the Zariski topology (\cref{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}), so a basic open \(D(r) \subseteq
2869	  q.X_i\) is a neighbourhood of that prime; hence \(\bigsqcup_{r \in G} D(r) = \top\), which is to say
2870	  \(\mathrm{span}\,G = (1)\). A unit-ideal membership uses only finitely many of the generators,
2871	  yielding a finite \(t \subseteq G\) with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = (1)\) and each \(D(r) \le q.X_i\) for
2872	  \(r \in t\).
2873	\end{proof}

890	\begin{lemma}[L5b.2 — Nagata change of variables: monic in the distinguished variable]
891	  \label{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
892	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
893	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
894	  % NOTE (variable convention): the project's univariate elimination uses
895	  %   MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv, which singles out variable X_0 as the polynomial
896	  %   variable over the coefficient ring MvPolynomial (Fin n) A. The choice of which of
897	  %   the d = n+1 variables is "distinguished/eliminated" is immaterial (a renaming);
898	  %   the informal prose elsewhere says "last variable X_d" but the Lean uses X_0.
899	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
900	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
901	  % the Nagata substitution is the standard device realising the support-dimension drop.
902	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
903	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
904	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
905	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
906	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
907	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g; map := MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A A_g).
908	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
909	  %       (F : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A) (hF : F ≠ 0) :
910	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
911	  %         (e : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A ≃ₐ[A] MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A),
912	  %         IsUnit
913	  %           (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv (Localization.Away g) n
914	  %             (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A (Localization.Away g)) (e F))).leadingCoeff
915	  %   The automorphism e is the triangular Nagata substitution; g is the (non-zero)
916	  %   leading coefficient that becomes a unit after inversion.
917	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
918	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
919	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
920	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
921	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
922	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
923	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).
924	\end{lemma}
925	
926	\begin{proof}
927	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
928	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
929	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
930	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
931	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
932	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
933	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
934	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
935	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
936	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
937	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
938	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
939	\end{proof}

967	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
968	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
969	  % the division algorithm in one variable is the standard mechanism of the support drop.
970	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
971	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
972	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
973	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
974	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (matches the landed decl verbatim).
975	  %   Finiteness is the `RingHom.Finite` of the composite ring map
976	  %   R[X_1,…,X_n] ↪ R[X_0,…,X_n] ↠ R[X_0,…,X_n]/(p)
977	  %   (constant inclusion X_j ↦ X_{j+1} = MvPolynomial.rename Fin.succ, then

979	  %     (R : Type*) [CommRing R] (n : ℕ)
980	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R)
981	  %       (hp : IsUnit (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p).leadingCoeff) :
982	  %       ((Ideal.Quotient.mk (Ideal.span {p})).comp
983	  %           (MvPolynomial.rename Fin.succ).toRingHom :
984	  %             MvPolynomial (Fin n) R →+* (MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R ⧸ Ideal.span {p})).Finite
985	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (single-variable elimination).}
986	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), and \(p \in R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) a
987	  polynomial whose image under the isomorphism
988	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) has a \emph{unit} leading
989	  coefficient — i.e.\ \(p\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to a unit. Then the
990	  quotient \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
991	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) (embedded by \(X_j \mapsto X_j\), \(j = 1, \dots, n\)).
992	\end{lemma}
993	
994	\begin{proof}
995	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
996	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (single-variable elimination).}
997	  Write \(S := R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) and let
998	  \(\Phi : R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } S[X_0]\) be the standard \(R\)-algebra
999	  isomorphism, which sends \(X_0\) to the univariate variable and embeds \(S\) as the constants. Put \(q := \Phi(p) \in S[X_0]\); by hypothesis its leading
1000	  coefficient \(u\) is a unit, so the associate \(\tilde q := u^{-1} \cdot q\) is monic and
1001	  \((\tilde q) = (q)\) in \(S[X_0]\). The isomorphism \(\Phi\) carries the ideal \((p)\) onto
1002	  \((q) = (\tilde q)\), hence descends to a ring isomorphism
1003	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p) \cong S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) that is \(S\)-linear, because \(S\) sits
1004	  inside both sides as the constants (respectively as the coefficient subring
1005	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\)). By \cref{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite} the quotient
1006	  \(S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) is module-finite over \(S\); transporting finiteness along the
1007	  \(S\)-linear isomorphism shows \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over
1008	  \(S = R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
1009	\end{proof}

1052	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
1053	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
1054	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
1055	  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
1056	  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1057	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
1058	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
1059	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
1060	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).

1063	\begin{proof}
1064	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
1065	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
1066	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
1067	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
1068	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1069	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
1070	  \(X_0\) is the one eliminated in the single-variable step; cf.\ \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
1071	
1072	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
1073	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
1074	
1075	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
1076	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
1077	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
1078	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
1079	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
1080	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
1081	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
1082	
1083	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
1084	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
1085	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
1086	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
1087	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
1088	  \(A_g\).
1089	
1090	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
1091	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
1092	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
1093	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
1094	
1095	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
1096	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
1097	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
1098	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
1099	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
1100	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
1101	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
1102	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
1103	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
1104	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
1105	
1106	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
1107	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
1108	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
1109	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
1110	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
1111	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
1112	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
1113	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
1114	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
1115	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
1116	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
1117	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
1118	
1119	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
1120	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
1121	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
1122	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
1123	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
1124	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
1125	
1126	  \emph{Action-diamond subtlety.} The conclusion of the lemma requires an
1127	  \(A_g\)-module structure on \(T_g\) sitting in a scalar tower
1128	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). One cannot simply install an anonymous
1129	  \(A_g\)-action by fiat: \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already carries its \emph{canonical}
1130	  localisation action of \(A_g\), and this canonical action is the one the scalar-tower
1131	  requirement sees. Hence the identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must be promoted to an
1132	  \(A_g\)-linear isomorphism — which it is, because the transported action agrees with the
1133	  canonical localisation action on the \(A_g\)-constants: the constant
1134	  \(\mathcal{C}\)-action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors through the original \(A\)-action on
1135	  \(T\) exactly as the canonical \(g\)-localisation action does. With the isomorphism made
1136	  \(A_g\)-linear, the \(R\)-module structure and finiteness pull back from \(T'_g\) to
1137	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
1138	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.
1139	
1140	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
1141	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
1142	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
1143	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
1144	\end{proof}
1145	
1146	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
1147	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
1148	
1149	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
1150	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
1151	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
1152	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
1153	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
1154	
1155	\begin{lemma}[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
1156	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
1157	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
1160	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
1161	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
1162	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
1163	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
1164	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
1165	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1166	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1167	\end{lemma}
1168	\begin{proof}
1169	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1170	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1171	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1172	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1173	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1174	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1175	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1176	\end{proof}
1177	
1178	\begin{lemma}[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1179	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1181	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1182	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1183	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1184	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1185	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1186	\end{lemma}
1187	\begin{proof}
1188	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1189	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1190	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1191	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1192	\end{proof}
1193	
1194	\begin{lemma}[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1195	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1196	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1197	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1198	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1199	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1200	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1201	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1202	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1203	\end{lemma}
1204	\begin{proof}
1205	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1206	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1207	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1208	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1209	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1210	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1211	  these conditions gives the claim.
1212	\end{proof}
1213	
1214	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1215	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}
1216	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated}
1217	  \mathlibok
1218	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1219	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1220	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1221	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1222	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1223	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).
1224	\end{lemma}
1225	
1226	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1227	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1228	  \lean{Module.Free.of_ringEquiv}
1229	  \mathlibok
1230	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1231	  Let \(R, R'\) be rings, \(M\) an \(R\)-module and \(M'\) an \(R'\)-module, and let
1232	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) be an additive isomorphism that is semilinear over a ring
1233	  isomorphism \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\), i.e.\ \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\).
1234	  If \(M\) is free over \(R\), then \(M'\) is free over \(R'\).
1235	\end{lemma}

1237	\begin{lemma}[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1238	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1240	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1241	  % LEAN DEPS: the localisation-transport primitives the Lean proof consumes are
1242	  %   IsLocalization.surj                          (clear the denominator of h over A_g)
1243	  %   IsLocalization.map_units                     (the cleared denominator s ∈ powers g is a unit)
1244	  %   IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated        (key ring identification: Away(g·a) on A_h)
1245	  %   IsLocalization.algEquiv                      (ring iso Away(g·a) ≅ A_h)
1246	  %   Module.Free.of_ringEquiv                     (transport freeness across that ring iso)
1247	  %   IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv                (the σ-semilinear module equivalence)
1248	  %   LinearEquiv.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization    (upgrade the R-linear equiv to Away(g·a)-linear)
1249	  % The one primitive NOT packaged by Mathlib — that the composite localisation map
1250	  %   ψ := mkLinearMap(powers h) ∘ mkLinearMap(powers g) : T → (T_g)_h
1251	  % satisfies IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ — is proved directly from the three
1252	  % IsLocalizedModule axioms (units act invertibly, surjectivity up to denominators, the
1253	  % equality criterion); there is no packaged localisation-of-localisation lemma to invoke.
1254	  % NOTE: the closed Lean proof DID find a packaged route — the composite-localisation
1255	  % `IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ` step goes through `IsBaseChange.comp` via
1256	  % `isLocalizedModule_iff_isBaseChange`, so the "no packaged lemma" remark above is superseded.
1257	  % WITNESS: the conclusion holds at the SINGLE product f := g·a (not at a square f²).
1258	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1259	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1260	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1261	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1262	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1263	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1264	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1265	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1266	\end{lemma}
1267	
1268	\begin{proof}
1269	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1270	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1271	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1272	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1273	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1274	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1275	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1276	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1277	
1278	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1279	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1280	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1281	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1282	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1283	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1284	
1285	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1286	  \[
1287	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1288	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1289	  \]
1290	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1291	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1292	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1293	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1294	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1295	
1296	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1297	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1298	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1299	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1300	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1301	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1302	\end{proof}

1304	\begin{lemma}[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1305	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
1306	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
1307	  % NOTE (signature): the Lean decl uses a SHARED universe
1308	  %   `(A N : Type u)` (not `(A : Type*) (N : Type*)`). This is load-bearing, not a
1309	  %   convenience: the base-domain-generalizing strong induction applies its IH at
1310	  %   `A_g = Localization.Away g`, and `LocalizedModule S M : Type (max u_R u_M)`, so the
1311	  %   reindexed module escapes `N`'s universe unless `A` and `N` share one. The geometric
1312	  %   target lives entirely in `Type u` (`Scheme.{u}`), so this is the natural setting.
1313	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1314	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1315	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1316	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1317	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
1318	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1319	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
1320	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
1321	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1322	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1323	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1324	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1325	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1326	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1327	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1328	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1329	  core of the induction).}
1330	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1331	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1332	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1333	
1334	\end{lemma}
1335	
1336	\begin{proof}
1337	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1338	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1339	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1340	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1341	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1342	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE: the induction must generalise the base domain A
1343	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
1344	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
1345	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
1346	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
1347	  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
1348	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
1349	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
1350	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
1351	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
1352	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
1353	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
1354	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
1355	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
1356	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
1357	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1358	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1359	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1360	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1361	
1362	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1363	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1364	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1365	
1366	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1367	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1368	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1369	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1370	  \begin{enumerate}
1371	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1372	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1373	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1374	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1375	      \(P_d\)-modules
1376	      \[
1377	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1378	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1379	      \]
1380	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1381	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1382	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1383	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1384	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1385	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1386	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1387	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1388	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1389	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1390	      to \(A_g\).
1391	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1392	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1393	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1394	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1395	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1396	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1397	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1398	      over \(A_f\).
1399	      % NOTE: the tower-descent helper lem:gf_away_tower_descent
1400	      % (free_localizationAway_of_away_tower) is now CLOSED and axiom-clean. The L5 sorry that
1401	      % remains is NOT a missing math step — it is a Lean elaboration blocker: the
1402	      % `OreLocalization` instance *presentations* on the localised quotient `(N ⧸ range φ)_g`
1403	      % (CommSemiring / AddCommMonoid / Module-SMul) emitted by `gf_torsion_reindex` are defeq
1404	      % but not instance-transparent-equal to the presentations the helper input expects. The
1405	      % clean fix is non-local: make `gf_torsion_reindex` emit its conclusion over the canonical
1406	      % `OreLocalization.*` instances (its final `exact` / existential binders), after which the
1407	      % recorded five-line assembly closes verbatim. Do NOT re-dispatch a raw L5 prover round
1408	      % without that instance-alignment refactor first.
1409	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1410	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1411	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1412	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1413	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1414	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1415	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1416	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1417	      completes the induction.
1418	  \end{enumerate}
1419	\end{proof}

1461	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1462	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1463	
1464	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1465	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1466	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1467	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1468	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1469	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1470	restricted sheaf over the base open. Neither is supplied directly by Mathlib: Mathlib
1471	does not provide the affine-local identification of \(\F|_W\) with the associated sheaf
1472	of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) together with the finiteness this transports.
1473	
1474	\begin{lemma}[G1 — finite-type quasi-coherent sheaf has finite affine sections]
1475	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1476	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1477	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1478	  % LEAN STATUS: G1 — project-built (mathlib-build target this iter); Mathlib has only
1479	  % the abstract SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType / IsQuasicoherent local-generator
1480	  % predicates, not this affine-local finiteness.
1481	  % NOTE: overlaps the QUOT keystone lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen — to be
1482	  % extracted to shared qcoh-affine-local infra (see STRATEGY Open questions).
1483	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB
1484	  % (Lemma "Characterizing modules of finite type", \S "Characterizing modules of
1485	  % finite type and finite presentation")
1486	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2092-L2098).
1487	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1488	  % "Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme.
1489	  %  The quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1490	  %  $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1491	  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."
1492	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1493	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of
1494	  modules with \(\F\) of finite type. Then for every affine open \(W \subseteq X\), the
1495	  section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1496	\end{lemma}
1497	
1498	\begin{proof}
1499	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1500	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (Tag 01PB, L2092-L2098;
1501	  % the Stacks proof reduces finite-type on a standard open cover to the algebra
1502	  % gluing lemma for finite generation of $M$ from finite generation of each $M_{f_i}$).
1503	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1504	  Fix an affine open \(W \subseteq X\) and write \(R = \Gamma(X, W)\) and
1505	  \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\). Identifying \(W\) with \(\Spec R\), quasi-coherence of \(\F\)
1506	  presents \(\F|_W\) as the associated sheaf \(\widetilde N\) of the \(R\)-module \(N\):
1507	  on a basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W\) the sections are the localized module
1508	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) = N_g\) over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = R_g\)
1509	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). The finite-type hypothesis on
1510	  \(\F\) says \(\F\) is locally generated by finitely many sections; restricted to the
1511	  quasi-compact affine \(W\), this yields a standard open cover
1512	  \(W = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} D(g_i)\) on each member of which \(\widetilde N|_{D(g_i)}\) is
1513	  generated by finitely many sections, i.e.\ each localization \(N_{g_i}\) is a finite
1514	  \(R_{g_i}\)-module. The elements \(g_1, \dots, g_n\) generate the unit ideal of \(R\)
1515	  (the \(D(g_i)\) cover \(\Spec R\)); by the standard finite-generation gluing
1516	  criterion — finite generation of \(N\) over \(R\) is local on such a cover — the
1517	  module \(N\) is finite over \(R\). Hence \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite
1518	  \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
1519	\end{proof}
1520	
1521	\begin{lemma}[G3 — flat-locality assembly from per-patch freeness]
1522	  \label{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1523	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1524	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1525	  % LEAN STATUS: G3 — project-built; the geometric glue identifying Γ(F, W∩W_j)
1526	  % localisations across the source cover and the base-restriction is the missing
1527	  % plumbing. Stub-level this iter; built after G1.
1528	  Let \(p : X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism with \(S\) integral and locally
1529	  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
1530	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
1531	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
1532	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
1533	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
1534	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
1535	  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
1536	  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
1537	\end{lemma}
1538	
1539	\begin{proof}
1540	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1541	  Free modules are flat (\(\OO\)-flatness of a free module), so each \((M_j)_f\) is flat
1542	  over \(A_f\). Flatness of a module is local on the source: a module is flat over a
1543	  ring iff it is flat after localizing at every maximal ideal, and the family
1544	  \(\{W_j\}\) covers \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), so the per-patch flatness statements assemble to
1545	  flatness of the section module of \(\F\) over \(A_f = \Gamma(S, V)\) on the whole
1546	  preimage \(p^{-1}(V)\). The flat locus is moreover stable under base-restriction along
1547	  the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow V\): for an affine \(U \le V\) the ring
1548	  \(\Gamma(S, U)\) is a localization of \(\Gamma(S, V) = A_f\) (an open immersion of
1549	  affines into a basic open being a localization), and for an affine \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\)
1550	  the module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is the corresponding localization of the section module
1551	  over the relevant patch \(W_j\) (via \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1552	  identifying the finite section modules and their localizations across the cover).
1553	  Flatness is preserved under localization of both the ring and the module, so
1554	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
1555	\end{proof}

1590	  % entail QuasiCompact; without it the statement is false (counterexample:

1592	  % promoting `LocallyOfFiniteType` to `FiniteType`, and there is no instance

1595	
1596	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1597	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1598	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1599	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.

1604	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1605	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1606	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1607	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1608	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.

1611	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).

1614	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1615	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1616	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1617	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1618	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1619	% by restricting to a non-empty
1620	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1621	\begin{proof}
1622	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
1623	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}

1625	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1626	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1627	
1628	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
1629	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
1630	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
1631	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
1632	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
1633	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1634	
1635	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1636	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1637	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
1638	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
1639	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
1640	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
1641	
1642	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1643	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1644	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1645	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1646	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1647	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1648	
1649	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1650	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
1651	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to
1652	  the fibrewise flatness conclusion, via the criterion that a module flat at every
1653	  maximal ideal is flat together with the locality of flatness on the source cover. This
1654	  produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1655	\end{proof}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).
136	\end{theorem}
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}
159	
160	\begin{definition}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Structure morphism of the relative spectrum]
164	  \label{def:relspec_structure_morphism}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.structureMorphism}
166	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
167	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
168	  The \emph{structure morphism}
169	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) is the canonical projection of the
170	  relative spectrum to its base, obtained as the colimit descent of the affine-local
171	  structure morphisms \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \to U\) over the directed affine open
172	  cover of \(X\). It is the morphism whose affineness and base-change behaviour are the
173	  subject of the universal-property and base-change results below.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\begin{proof}
177	
178	\leanok
179	  The structure morphism is the base morphism of the relative gluing data of
180	  \(\mathcal{A}\); it is the projection underlying \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
181	\end{proof}

2900	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Equivalence}).}
2901	  Let \(e : C \simeq D\) be an equivalence of categories carrying Grothendieck topologies \(J\)
2902	  on \(C\) and \(K\) on \(D\), and suppose \(e.\mathrm{inverse}\) is a dense subsite for
2903	  \((K, J)\). Then for any target category \(A\) the sheaf categories are equivalent:
2904	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}(J, A) \simeq \mathrm{Sheaf}(K, A)\).
2905	\end{lemma}

2907	\begin{lemma}[Module sheaves transport across an equivalence of ringed sites]
2908	  \label{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}
2909	  \lean{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}
2910	  \mathlibok
2911	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2912	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.PushforwardContinuous}).}
2913	  Let \(e : C \simeq D\) be an equivalence of sites with both \(e.\mathrm{functor}\) and
2914	  \(e.\mathrm{inverse}\) continuous, and let \(S\) (a sheaf of rings on \(J\)) and \(R\) (a sheaf
2915	  of rings on \(K\)) be related by mutually inverse comparison maps across the continuous
2916	  pushforwards. Then the categories of sheaves of modules are equivalent,
2917	  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules}\,R \simeq \mathrm{SheafOfModules}\,S\), compatibly with the
2918	  underlying ring-sheaf identification. This is the module-level lift used in step~3 of
2919	  \cref{lem:over_restrict_iso}.
2920	\end{lemma}
2921	
2922	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2923	[The over-equivalence functor is cocontinuous]
2924	  \label{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isCocontinuous}
2925	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.overEquivalence_functor_isCocontinuous}
2926	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
2927	  \textit{Topological layer of bridge C; fills the \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} TODO.}
2928	  The functor of \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib})
2929	  is cocontinuous (cover-lifting) from the \(U\)-slice \(J_X|_U\) of the opens Grothendieck
2930	  topology of \(X\) to the opens Grothendieck topology \(J_U\) of the subspace \(U\).
2931	\end{lemma}
2932	\begin{proof}
2933	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
2934	  A sieve covers in the slice topology \(J_X|_U\) iff its image sieve covers in \(J_X\), and
2935	  covering for the opens topology is pointwise: a sieve covers iff its members' opens cover the
2936	  base set-theoretically. Given a point \(x\) of a slice object and a cover member in \(J_U\)
2937	  containing the image of \(x\), transport it across the open embedding \(U \hookrightarrow X\),
2938	  which is injective: the set-theoretic image of an open \(V \subseteq U\) is an open of \(X\)
2939	  contained in the base, a neighbourhood of \(x\) lying in the image sieve. This witnesses the
2940	  cover-lifting condition.
2941	\end{proof}
2942	
2943	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2944	[The over-equivalence inverse is cocontinuous]
2945	  \label{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isCocontinuous}
2946	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.overEquivalence_inverse_isCocontinuous}
2947	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
2948	  \textit{Topological layer of bridge C; fills the \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} TODO.}
2949	  Symmetrically, the inverse of \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) is cocontinuous from the
2950	  opens topology \(J_U\) of the subspace \(U\) to the \(U\)-slice \(J_X|_U\).
2951	\end{lemma}
2952	\begin{proof}
2953	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
2954	  As in \cref{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isCocontinuous}, reduce to the pointwise covering
2955	  criterion and the over-sieve image criterion; given a point and a slice-cover member, pull the
2956	  member back along the embedding \(U \hookrightarrow X\) (preimage of an open of \(X\) is an open
2957	  of the subspace) to obtain a cover member of \(J_U\) witnessing the lift.
2958	\end{proof}

2960	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2961	[The over-equivalence inverse is a dense subsite]
2962	  \label{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isDenseSubsite}
2963	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.overEquivalence_inverse_isDenseSubsite}
2964	  \uses{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isCocontinuous, lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isCocontinuous}
2965	  \textit{Topological layer of bridge C; the dense-subsite witness assembled from the two
2966	  cocontinuities.}
2967	  The inverse of \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) is a dense subsite for \((J_U, J_X|_U)\).
2968	  This is formal: an equivalence both of whose legs are cocontinuous
2969	  (\cref{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isCocontinuous},
2970	  \cref{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isCocontinuous}) yields a dense subsite on its inverse.
2971	\end{lemma}

2973	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2974	[The over-equivalence functor is continuous]
2975	  \label{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous}
2976	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous}
2977	  \uses{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isCocontinuous}
2978	  \textit{Topological layer of bridge C; needed for the module-level lift of step~3.}
2979	  The functor of \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) is continuous (cover-preserving) for
2980	  \((J_X|_U, J_U)\). It is obtained from the cocontinuity of the inverse
2981	  (\cref{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isCocontinuous}) through the equivalence adjunction: the
2982	  left adjoint of a cocontinuous functor is continuous.
2983	\end{lemma}

2996	\begin{definition}\leanok
2997	[The over-site/open-subspace sheaf equivalence]
2998	  \label{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}
2999	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.overEquivalence_sheafCongr}
3000	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib, lem:equivalence_sheafCongr_mathlib,
3001	    lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isDenseSubsite}
3002	  \textit{Keystone of the topological layer of bridge C.}
3003	  For any category \(A\), the site equivalence \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\)
3004	  (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}) lifts to an equivalence of sheaf categories
3005	  \[
3006	    \mathrm{Sheaf}(J_X|_U,\, A) \;\simeq\; \mathrm{Sheaf}(J_U,\, A),
3007	  \]
3008	  obtained by feeding the dense-subsite witness
3009	  (\cref{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isDenseSubsite}) to the general sheaf-congruence
3010	  construction (\cref{lem:equivalence_sheafCongr_mathlib}). This is exactly the equivalence left
3011	  as a TODO in Mathlib's \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean}, and it is the object across which

3015	% --- (C, step 3) The module-level equivalence and its functor identification ---
3016	
3017	% The two blocks below are the module-category layer assembled on top of the topological
3018	% sheaf-congruence def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr: the step-3 equivalence of categories of sheaves of
3019	% modules (def:over_restrict_equiv) and the step-4 functor identification (lem:over_restrict_functor_iso)
3020	% whose object component is the slice-touching bridge lem:over_restrict_iso below. They are

3022	
3023	\begin{definition}
3024	
3025	\leanok
3026	[The slice-to-geometric module-category equivalence (gap1, C, step 3)]
3027	  \label{def:over_restrict_equiv}
3028	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictEquiv}
3029	  \uses{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr, lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib,
3030	    lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous,
3031	    lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}
3032	  \textit{Module-level lift of the topological bridge C; the step-3 equivalence on which the
3033	  slice-touching isomorphism is built.}
3034	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open. The category of sheaves of modules over the
3035	  sliced structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) on the slice site \(J.\mathrm{over}\,U\) is
3036	  equivalent to the category \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}.\mathrm{Modules}\) of sheaves of modules on the open
3037	  subscheme \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\):
3038	  \[
3039	    \mathrm{SheafOfModules}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\bigr)
3040	      \;\simeq\; U.\mathrm{toScheme}.\mathrm{Modules}.
3041	  \]
3042	  This is the module-category lift of the topological sheaf equivalence
3043	  \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}.
3044	\end{definition}
3045	\begin{proof}
3046	  \uses{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr, lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib,
3047	    lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous,
3048	    lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}
3049	  Apply the module-sheaf transport \(\mathrm{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\)
3050	  (\cref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}) to the site equivalence
3051	  \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}), whose two legs are
3052	  both continuous (\cref{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous},
3053	  \cref{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}). The transport consumes a pair of mutually inverse
3054	  ring-sheaf comparison morphisms between the sliced structure sheaf
3055	  \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) and the geometric structure sheaf
3056	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U.\mathrm{toScheme}}\); for these one takes the whiskered (co)unit of the site
3057	  equivalence \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) into the structure presheaf, paired with the
3058	  identity. The identity is legal precisely because the geometric structure sheaf is
3059	  the transport of the sliced one across the ring-valued instance of
3060	  \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr} (this is step~2 of the bridge, which holds by
3061	  construction). Orienting the resulting equivalence so that its functor runs from the slice
3062	  side to the geometric side gives the stated equivalence.
3063	\end{proof}

3065	\begin{lemma}
3066	
3067	\leanok
3068	[Functor identification of the slice-to-geometric equivalence (gap1, C, step 4)]
3069	  \label{lem:over_restrict_functor_iso}
3070	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictFunctorIso}
3071	  \uses{def:over_restrict_equiv, lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib,
3072	    lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
3073	  \textit{The composite of the slice functor with the step-3 equivalence is the geometric restriction
3074	  functor.}
3075	  With \(X\) and \(U \subseteq X\) as above, the composite of the abstract Grothendieck-slice functor
3076	  \(M \mapsto M.\mathrm{over}\,U\) (pushforward along the identity of the sliced ring sheaf) with the
3077	  functor of the step-3 equivalence (\cref{def:over_restrict_equiv}) is naturally isomorphic to the
3078	  geometric restriction functor along the open immersion \(U.\iota\):
3079	  \[
3080	    \bigl(\mathrm{pushforward}\,\mathbf{1}\bigr) \;\circ\; (\mathrm{overRestrictEquiv}\,U).\mathrm{functor}
3081	      \;\cong\; \mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota
3082	  \]
3083	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}).
3084	\end{lemma}
3085	\begin{proof}
3086	  \uses{def:over_restrict_equiv, lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib,
3087	    lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
3088	  Both sides are pushforwards of sheaves of modules along the \emph{same} opens functor. Indeed the
3089	  opens functor of the open immersion factors as
3090	  \[
3091	    U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}
3092	      \;=\; (\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U).\mathrm{inverse}\;\circ\;\mathrm{Over.forget}\,U
3093	  \]
3094	  (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}), an equality that holds by construction, and
3095	  \(\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota\) (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}) is precisely
3096	  pushforward along \(U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}\). The left-hand composite is pushforward along the
3097	  factored functor on the right. Identify the two via the composition law for module-sheaf
3098	  pushforward (\(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}\)) followed by congruence of pushforward along the equal ring
3099	  morphisms (\(\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\)); the required ring-morphism equality is routine.
3100	\end{proof}

3121	  \textit{The one lemma that must touch the Grothendieck slice site; everything downstream of it is

3123	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open. The \emph{abstract Grothendieck-slice}
3124	  restriction \(M.\mathrm{over}\,U\) — an object of the category of sheaves of modules over the

3141	    lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib,
3142	    lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
3143	  The isomorphism is built in four steps, ascending from the purely topological layer to the
3144	  module-level statement.
3145	
3146	  \emph{Step 1 (topological layer --- done).} The opens functor of the open immersion
3147	  \(U.\iota\) realizes the slice of the opens site of \(X\) over \(U\) as the opens site of the
3148	  subspace \(U\); on sheaf categories this is the equivalence
3149	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}(J_X|_U,\,A) \simeq \mathrm{Sheaf}(J_U,\,A)\) of
3150	  \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}, for every target category \(A\). This is the
3151	  topos-theoretic layer, assembled from the cocontinuity/continuity of both legs of
3152	  \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}).
3153	
3154	  \emph{Step 2 (geometric ring-sheaf identification --- the current obstacle).} Identify the
3155	  sliced structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) with the structure sheaf
3156	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U}\) of the open subscheme \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\), transported across the
3157	  \(\mathrm{RingCat}\)-valued instance of \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}; this is an
3158	  isomorphism of sheaves of rings. The bridge is the factorization of the opens functor of the
3159	  immersion,
3160	  \[
3161	    U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}
3162	      \;=\; (\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U).\mathrm{inverse}
3163	        \;\circ\; \mathrm{Over.forget}\,U ,
3164	  \]
3165	  under which the structure-sheaf comparison reduces to the standard open-immersion
3166	  structure-sheaf identifications; recall that \(\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota\)
3167	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}) is precisely pushforward along
3168	  \(U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}\). This ring-sheaf identification is the remaining geometric
3169	  obstacle.
3170	
3171	  \emph{Step 3 (lift to modules).} Given the step-2 identification of the underlying ring sheaves
3172	  and the continuity of both legs (\cref{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous},
3173	  \cref{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}), lift the sheaf-of-rings comparison to an
3174	  equivalence of categories of sheaves of modules via
3175	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\)
3176	  (\cref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}). Under this equivalence the sliced
3177	  module \(M.\mathrm{over}\,U\) corresponds to the geometric restriction
3178	  \((\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota).\mathrm{obj}\,M\).
3179	
3180	  \emph{Step 4 (compose).} Compose with
3181	  \(\mathrm{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib})
3182	  to land the isomorphism \(M.\mathrm{over}\,U \cong M.\mathrm{restrict}\,U.\iota \cong
3183	  U.\iota^{*}M\). The two sides live a priori in different module categories, so the literal Lean
3184	  statement of \(\mathrm{overRestrictIso}\) is phrased through the step-3 equivalence functor; for
3185	  \(X = \Spec R\) and \(U = D(r)\) the target is \((\Spec R_r).\mathrm{Modules}\) under
3186	  \(D(r) \cong \Spec R_r\) (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}).
3187	\end{proof}
